Original_FR: "if the item the user wants is available for exchange, the user can request the exchange of items that he/she has with the item he/she wants."
Conflicting_FR: Users can request exchanges only if the items are unavailable.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended meaning of "unavailable" in Requirement B—replace it with "not currently owned by the user" to align with Requirement A’s intent that exchanges occur only for items the user doesn’t already have.  
2. Revise Requirement B to state: “Users can request exchanges only for items they do not already possess,” ensuring consistency with Requirement A’s condition of exchanging owned items for desired ones.  
3. Merge both requirements into a single, unambiguous statement: “A user may request an exchange of an item they own for a desired item only if the desired item is available for exchange and not already in their possession.”

Original_FR: "if the user no longer wishes to sell/exchange the item, he/she can remove the item from the system by touching a??Remove the itema?? button."
Conflicting_FR: Items shall remain in the system even if the user clicks "Remove the item".
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior: Determine whether "removal" means soft deletion (item hidden from user view but retained in the system for audit or recovery) or hard deletion (permanent removal). Update both requirements to reflect this decision consistently.  
2. Introduce a two-tier removal process: Allow users to "hide" or "deactivate" items via the "Remove the item" button (satisfying Requirement A), while retaining the data in the system per Requirement B, with clear labeling that the item is no longer publicly listed.  
3. Add a retention policy clause: Modify Requirement B to specify that items are retained only for a defined period (e.g., 30 days) after removal, enabling eventual deletion and aligning with user expectations in Requirement A.

Original_FR: "if the user no longer wishes to use the system, he/she can click a??Delete Profilea?? button. After deleting the profile, the user will no longer be able to use the system with the account details that he entered before."
Conflicting_FR: The system shall retain user account and allow login even after clicking "Delete Profile".
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior with stakeholders: Determine whether "Delete Profile" should permanently remove account access (Requirement A) or merely deactivate/hide the profile while preserving login capability (Requirement B).  
2. Refactor the functionality into two distinct actions: Offer both “Deactivate Profile” (retains account for future login, per Requirement B) and “Permanently Delete Profile” (irreversibly removes access, per Requirement A).  
3. Update terminology and system behavior consistently: If deletion must be reversible, rename the button to “Deactivate” or “Disable Profile” and adjust Requirement A accordingly to align with Requirement B.

Original_FR: 10000 users should be able to use the system at the same time.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall restrict the number of concurrent users to a maximum of 5000 to maintain response time and data consistency.
Resolution:
1. Reconcile the requirements by defining different user tiers or service levels—allow up to 10,000 concurrent users with basic functionality while restricting high-intensity operations (e.g., data writes) to a subset of 5,000 users to preserve performance and consistency.  
2. Investigate and document the technical constraints behind Requirement B; if feasible, invest in system scalability (e.g., improved architecture, caching, or database optimization) to safely support 10,000 concurrent users without compromising response time or data consistency.  
3. Clarify stakeholder priorities through negotiation—determine whether supporting 10,000 users (scalability) or guaranteeing performance/consistency at 5,000 users is more critical, and update one requirement accordingly based on business goals.

Original_FR: 10000 users should be able to use the system at the same time.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall allow administrative concurrent access only once per system startup and revoke all administrative sessions automatically within 5 seconds after they begin.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement B applies only to privileged administrative sessions, while Requirement A refers to general user sessions, and explicitly separate the two user types in the system architecture.  
2. Modify Requirement B to allow a limited number of concurrent administrative sessions (e.g., up to 5) with automatic revocation after 5 seconds, balancing security and operational needs without impacting the 10,000-user capacity.  
3. Implement session prioritization or queuing for administrative access so that only one admin session is active at a time, while ensuring this mechanism does not interfere with the scalability required for 10,000 concurrent regular users.

Original_FR: A CEP card may contain currencies that the load acquirer does not support. As a result, load devices must use the alphabetic issuer-supplied currency code and currency exponent from the CEP slot(s) to display the source amounts for the currency conversion transaction.
Conflicting_FR: Load devices shall reject any CEP card containing currencies not supported by the load acquirer, and no source amount shall be displayed.
Resolution:
1. Align both requirements by updating Requirement A to state that load devices must only process and display source amounts for currencies supported by the load acquirer, removing the allowance for unsupported currencies.  
2. Modify Requirement B to permit display of source amounts using issuer-supplied currency codes (per Requirement A) while still rejecting transaction processing for unsupported currencies, thereby separating display logic from transaction approval.  
3. Introduce a system configuration flag that allows the load acquirer to choose between strict rejection (Requirement B) or informational display with rejection (Requirement A), ensuring consistent behavior based on operational policy.

Original_FR: A class shall be either a non-clinical class or a clinical class.
Conflicting_FR: A class may contain both clinical and non-clinical components within the same structure.
Resolution:
1. Clarify terminology by defining “class” consistently—either as a logical grouping (allowing mixed components) or as a categorization tag (mutually exclusive types)—and align both requirements to the same definition.  
2. Refine Requirement A to state that a class must be primarily classified as either clinical or non-clinical based on its dominant component type, while allowing minor components of the other type as permitted by Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a hierarchical model where a top-level class is strictly clinical or non-clinical (satisfying Requirement A), but may contain subcomponents or subclasses of the opposite type (accommodating Requirement B).

Original_FR: A CND as defined in clause 3 may use the CNG to access NGN services.
Conflicting_FR: A CND shall not be allowed to access NGN services through the CNG interface for security reasons.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "CND" in Requirement A by specifying that only CNDs meeting defined security criteria (e.g., certified or trusted devices) may use the CNG to access NGN services, aligning with Requirement B’s security intent.  
2. Revise Requirement A to exclude access to sensitive NGN services via CNG, permitting only non-sensitive or anonymized services, thereby satisfying the security restriction in Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a conditional clause in both requirements stating that CNG-mediated access is permitted only when additional security controls (e.g., authentication, encryption, or audit logging) are implemented and verified.

Original_FR: A cohort shall be associated to one Program of Study offered by the department.
Conflicting_FR: A cohort may be associated with multiple Programs of Study across departments.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "Program of Study" by distinguishing between primary and secondary affiliations, allowing a cohort to have one primary Program of Study (per Requirement A) while optionally linking to additional programs across departments (satisfying Requirement B).  
2. Introduce a hierarchy or categorization of cohorts—e.g., “departmental cohorts” bound to a single program (per Requirement A) and “cross-departmental cohorts” permitted to associate with multiple programs (per Requirement B)—based on institutional use cases.  
3. Revise Requirement A to specify that a cohort must be associated with at least one Program of Study offered by a department, while explicitly permitting additional associations with other programs, thereby harmonizing both requirements.

Original_FR: A cohort shall be associated to one Program of Study offered by the department.
Conflicting_FR: A cohort's Program of Study may be changed only once per calendar year and any change must be applied within a 10-second transaction window; changes attempted outside this window shall be rejected even if performed by DEPARTMENT_ADMIN.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to allow a cohort to be associated with only one Program of Study at any given time, while explicitly permitting changes under the constraints defined in Requirement B.  
2. Modify Requirement B to specify that the once-per-year change restriction applies only after the cohort has been initially assigned, ensuring alignment with Requirement A’s intent of single-program association at any point in time.  
3. Add an exception clause to Requirement B allowing immediate correction of erroneous initial assignments (e.g., within 24 hours of creation) without counting toward the annual change limit, preserving data integrity while maintaining operational flexibility.

Original_FR: A CPN user shall be able to configure any unmanaged service provided to the user.
Conflicting_FR: A CPN user shall not be allowed to configure unmanaged services without administrator approval.
Resolution:
1. Refine Requirement A to specify that users can configure only those unmanaged services explicitly permitted by system policy or pre-approved by an administrator.  
2. Introduce a role-based access control mechanism where “power users” with delegated privileges may configure certain unmanaged services without full administrator approval, while standard users require approval.  
3. Implement a workflow where user-initiated configuration changes to unmanaged services are queued for administrator review and approval before being applied, satisfying both user intent and administrative oversight.

Original_FR: A current temperature value that is received from an individual thermostat shall be compared to the valid temperature range values.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall not perform validation against predefined range values for temperature inputs.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of Requirement B by specifying that it applies only to user-entered temperature inputs, not to sensor data from thermostats, thereby allowing Requirement A to remain valid for automated device inputs.  
2. Revise Requirement A to exclude range validation if Requirement B is intended to apply universally, and instead implement anomaly detection or logging without explicit range-based rejection.  
3. Introduce a configurable validation policy that allows enabling or disabling range checks per input source (e.g., enabled for thermostats per Requirement A, disabled for manual inputs per Requirement B).

Original_FR: A description and time/date of each prescription that has been ordered or given is displayed starting with most recent.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall display prescriptions in chronological order from oldest to newest.
Resolution:
1. Clarify user needs through stakeholder consultation to determine whether clinical workflows prioritize recency (e.g., for review) or chronological progression (e.g., for treatment history), and align both requirements accordingly.  
2. Implement a default view that satisfies the primary use case (e.g., most recent first) while providing a user-controlled toggle or sort option to switch to chronological order (oldest to newest).  
3. Refine the requirements to specify context-specific ordering—for example, display prescriptions in reverse chronological order on summary screens but in chronological order within detailed treatment timelines.

Original_FR: A failed login will alert the physician/pharmacist.
Conflicting_FR: A failed login attempt shall not generate alerts to users for security reasons.
Resolution:
1. Restrict alerts to only authorized security personnel or system administrators instead of end users like physicians/pharmacists, satisfying both security concerns and the need for notification.  
2. Implement a delayed or aggregated alerting mechanism (e.g., daily summary of failed attempts) that informs relevant staff without revealing real-time login failures that could aid attackers.  
3. Differentiate between types of failed login attempts—e.g., alert on repeated or suspicious failures while suppressing alerts for isolated user errors—to balance usability and security.

Original_FR: A failed login will alert the physician/pharmacist.
Conflicting_FR: Login failure alerts shall be delivered only once to Security Administrators and thereafter be suppressed for 24 hours, regardless of subsequent failures.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "alert" in Requirement A by specifying that alerts to physicians/pharmacists are limited to failures on their own accounts, while systemic or repeated failures are handled exclusively by Security Administrators per Requirement B.  
2. Modify Requirement B to allow distinct alerting channels: suppress repeated alerts to Security Administrators after the first, but permit user-specific alerts (e.g., to the affected physician/pharmacist) for each failed attempt without suppression.  
3. Introduce a tiered alerting policy where initial login failures notify the end user (physician/pharmacist), while patterns indicating potential security threats (e.g., multiple failures across accounts) trigger a single alert to Security Administrators, aligning both requirements with their respective intents.

Original_FR: A flight pattern shall document the longitude latitude and altitude of waypoints in a flight route as offsets from the starting position of the UAV.
Conflicting_FR: A flight pattern shall document the magnetic bearing and distance of waypoints in a flight route as offsets from the starting position of the UAV.
Resolution:
1. Define a single canonical representation (e.g., latitude/longitude/altitude) for waypoint offsets and derive magnetic bearing and distance computationally when needed, ensuring consistency and traceability.  
2. Allow both representations in the flight pattern specification but require that they be mutually consistent and synchronized through automated validation checks.  
3. Introduce separate, clearly scoped sub-requirements: one for navigation systems requiring geodetic coordinates (Requirement A) and another for operator interfaces using polar coordinates (Requirement B), with a defined transformation layer between them.

Original_FR: A flight pattern shall document the longitude latitude and altitude of waypoints in a flight route as offsets from the starting position of the UAV.
Conflicting_FR: A flight pattern shall record waypoints using absolute geographic coordinates rather than offsets.
Resolution:
1. Choose a single coordinate representation (either absolute or offset-based) based on system needs—e.g., use absolute coordinates if interoperability with external systems is required, or offsets if relative navigation simplifies onboard processing.  
2. Support both representations by defining a conversion mechanism: store waypoints in one format internally and provide an interface to translate to the other when needed.  
3. Clarify the scope of each requirement—e.g., specify that Requirement A applies to internal flight control logic while Requirement B applies to data exchange or logging interfaces.

Original_FR: A list of all dispute cases that meet the entered search criteria must be provided to the user. The list of cases must differentiate the type and/or status of the case by color-coding the line item in the list.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall provide a textual indication of case status instead of using color-coding.
Resolution:
1. Replace color-coding with a textual label (e.g., "[Open]", "[Closed]") next to each case while retaining the full list functionality to satisfy both requirements.  
2. Implement both color-coding and textual indicators, but make color-coding optional or supplementary (e.g., for accessibility compliance, ensure text is primary).  
3. Clarify stakeholder priorities through a requirements workshop to determine whether accessibility (favoring text) or visual efficiency (favoring color) takes precedence, then update one requirement accordingly.

Original_FR: A list of all dispute cases that meet the entered search criteria must be provided to the user. The list of cases must differentiate the type and/or status of the case by color-coding the line item in the list.
Conflicting_FR: The list of dispute cases shall differentiate type/status using textual tags instead of color-coding.
Resolution:
1. Replace color-coding with textual tags as the primary differentiation method to satisfy Requirement B, and optionally retain subtle color cues as a secondary visual aid for users without color vision deficiencies, ensuring compliance with accessibility standards.  
2. Implement a user-configurable display preference allowing users to choose between color-coding, textual tags, or both, thereby satisfying both requirements through personalization.  
3. Use textual tags as the mandatory differentiation method (per Requirement B) and enhance them with accessible, non-color-dependent visual indicators (e.g., icons or patterns) to maintain usability without relying solely on color.

Original_FR: A list of available patients is displayed after login.
Conflicting_FR: The list of available patients shall be accessible only after explicit user request, not automatically after login.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to state that a list of available patients is displayed only after the user explicitly requests it (e.g., by clicking a button or selecting a menu option), aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Introduce a user preference setting during or after login that allows users to choose whether the patient list appears automatically or only on demand, satisfying both requirements through configurability.  
3. Clarify the term "displayed" in Requirement A to mean the list is loaded in the background but not shown in the UI until explicitly requested, ensuring no automatic visual presentation while maintaining system readiness.

Original_FR: A meeting scheduler system will inform the ???potential meeting participant???? of the meeting.
Conflicting_FR: The meeting scheduler shall notify only confirmed attendees, excluding tentative participants.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the terminology in Requirement A by replacing “potential meeting participant” with a precise term (e.g., “confirmed attendee”) to align with Requirement B’s scope.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that notifications are sent in stages: initial invitations to potential participants, followed by final confirmations only to confirmed attendees, thereby satisfying both informational and privacy needs.  
3. Introduce a configurable notification policy in the system that allows organizers to choose whether tentative participants receive updates, ensuring flexibility while respecting Requirement B as a default or optional rule.

Original_FR: A meeting scheduler system will inform the potential meeting participant of the meeting.
Conflicting_FR: The meeting scheduler system shall not automatically notify participants until the meeting is confirmed.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to specify that the system informs participants only after the meeting is confirmed, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Modify the system design to send a tentative notification (e.g., “proposed meeting”) without confirming attendance, satisfying early awareness while respecting the no-auto-confirmation rule.  
3. Introduce a user-configurable setting allowing organizers to choose whether to notify participants immediately or only upon confirmation, accommodating both requirements through flexibility.

Original_FR: A meeting scheduler system will temporarily reserve the ???time slots???? for the proposed meeting.
Conflicting_FR: The scheduler shall not reserve time slots until the meeting is finalized.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the meaning of “reserve” in both requirements—distinguish between a soft hold (tentative reservation visible only to organizers) and a hard reservation (blocking the slot for others)—and align terminology accordingly.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that the system may place a *tentative* hold on time slots during scheduling, while Requirement B is updated to prohibit *confirmed* reservations until finalization.  
3. Introduce a time-bound tentative reservation mechanism: allow temporary holds for a limited duration (e.g., 15 minutes) that auto-expire if the meeting isn’t finalized, satisfying both the need for coordination and the constraint against premature commitment.

Original_FR: A MODIS Computer Software Configuration Item (CSCI) is any independently tracked and configured unit of software within the MODIS SDP S/W, including processes, scripts, and utility libraries.  Each CSCI in the MODIS SDP S/W shall run in the ECS production environment.
Conflicting_FR: Access to MODIS CSCI configuration data shall be permitted to MODIS_SYSTEM_ADMIN holders only during a predefined maintenance window of 2 minutes per week; outside that 2-minute window no access or modification is allowed.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that "run in the ECS production environment" (Requirement A) refers only to execution, not to runtime configuration access—ensuring that normal operation does not require configuration changes outside the maintenance window.  
2. Extend or schedule the 2-minute maintenance window (Requirement B) to align with actual operational needs for CSCI deployment, updates, or recovery, ensuring it supports reliable execution in the production environment.  
3. Introduce an exception mechanism in Requirement B allowing emergency configuration access under controlled, audited conditions without violating the intent of restricted access.

Original_FR: A MODIS Computer Software Configuration Item (CSCI) is any independently tracked and configured unit of software within the MODIS SDP S/W, including processes, scripts, and utility libraries.  Each CSCI within the MODIS SDP S/W systemshall employ the Earth Science Data and Information System (ESDIS)-supplied SDPTK mandatory functions to access the DAAC production environment and services.
Conflicting_FR: Use of ESDIS SDPTK functions shall be rate-limited to one invocation per 24 hours per DAAC administrator; any additional attempts within the period are rejected even if initiated by DAAC admins.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement B applies only to administrative or non-operational uses of SDPTK functions, while allowing MODIS CSCI operational invocations (as mandated by Requirement A) to be exempt from the 24-hour rate limit.  
2. Modify Requirement B to define separate rate limits for system-level software (e.g., MODIS CSCIs) versus human-initiated administrative actions, ensuring operational continuity without compromising resource governance.  
3. Introduce a role-based access control mechanism in the SDPTK that distinguishes between automated CSCI processes and DAAC administrator actions, applying the 24-hour limit only to the latter.

Original_FR: A MODIS CSCI ... shall be coded according to the standards specified in SDST-022.
Conflicting_FR: Each CSCI may be coded according to team-specific internal guidelines if equivalent to SDST-022.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that "equivalent to SDST-022" in Requirement B must be formally verified and approved by a designated authority (e.g., software quality assurance) to ensure true equivalence before allowing deviations.  
2. Revise Requirement A to explicitly permit the use of team-specific guidelines when they are demonstrably equivalent to SDST-022, thereby aligning both requirements under a unified compliance framework.  
3. Define objective criteria or a checklist for assessing equivalence to SDST-022, ensuring consistent interpretation and reducing ambiguity between teams and stakeholders.

Original_FR: A MODIS CSCI ... shall employ ESDIS-supplied SDPTK mandatory functions to access the DAAC environment.
Conflicting_FR: A CSCI may use custom-developed functions to access DAAC services instead of ESDIS-supplied SDPTK.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "mandatory" in Requirement A by specifying whether exceptions are permitted for performance, compatibility, or operational reasons, thereby aligning with the flexibility implied in Requirement B.  
2. Revise Requirement B to state that custom-developed functions may only be used if they fully replicate the interface and behavior of the ESDIS-supplied SDPTK mandatory functions and receive formal approval from the ESDIS authority.  
3. Consolidate both requirements into a single statement that mandates use of SDPTK functions by default but allows approved deviations under defined conditions (e.g., documented justification, testing, and stakeholder agreement).

Original_FR: A MODIS CSCI ... shall run in the ECS production environment.
Conflicting_FR: Each CSCI shall run in a controlled test environment before deployment to ECS production.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement A applies only after successful testing, by amending it to: “A MODIS CSCI shall run in the ECS production environment only after completing validation in a controlled test environment.”  
2. Explicitly sequence the requirements by adding a dependency clause to Requirement B stating that deployment to ECS production (per Requirement A) is contingent upon successful execution in the controlled test environment.  
3. Define “run” contextually—interpret Requirement A as referring to operational execution and Requirement B as referring to pre-deployment validation—to eliminate ambiguity and ensure both can coexist without contradiction.

Original_FR: A MySQL database will be used to store all information required to answer questions.
Conflicting_FR: A PostgreSQL database shall be used as the primary storage for all question-answer data.
Resolution:
1. Clarify stakeholder priorities and select a single database system (either MySQL or PostgreSQL) based on technical requirements, team expertise, and long-term maintainability.  
2. Refactor the requirements to allow for database abstraction (e.g., using an ORM or data access layer), enabling flexibility to support either MySQL or PostgreSQL without hardcoding the choice.  
3. If both databases must be used, define a clear data synchronization strategy and specify which system serves as the source of truth versus a secondary or replicated store.

Original_FR: A PGE ... Each PGE shall be provided with one or more sets of production rules specifying inputs, outputs, and activation conditions.
Conflicting_FR: A PGE shall execute dynamically without predefined production rules.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "predefined production rules" in Requirement B—allow dynamic rule generation or loading at runtime while still requiring that at least one valid rule set exists (as per Requirement A) before execution begins.  
2. Refactor Requirement A to permit optional initial rule sets, enabling PGEs to start without predefined rules but allowing rules to be provided or discovered dynamically during execution.  
3. Introduce a configuration mode where PGEs can operate in either "rule-defined" (satisfying Requirement A) or "fully dynamic" (satisfying Requirement B) modes, with the mode selection governed by system context or deployment needs.

Original_FR: A PGE ... shall not set or redefine environmental variables.
Conflicting_FR: A PGE shall be permitted to modify environmental variables during execution for configuration purposes.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "environmental variables" by distinguishing between system-wide environment variables (which must not be modified) and process-local or temporary variables (which may be used for internal configuration).  
2. Revise Requirement A to allow controlled modification of a predefined, safe subset of environment variables explicitly approved for PGE configuration purposes.  
3. Introduce a runtime sandboxing mechanism that permits PGEs to set environment variables within an isolated context without affecting the broader system environment.

Original_FR: A PGE ... The SDST shall integrate all software processes into PGEs executing all processes in the MODIS TLCF.
Conflicting_FR: The SDST shall integrate only selected software processes into PGEs, not all processes.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of “all software processes” in Requirement A by defining which processes are mandatory for integration versus optional, aligning with the selective approach in Requirement B.  
2. Revise Requirement A to specify that only those software processes relevant to the MODIS TLCF shall be integrated into PGEs, thereby reconciling with Requirement B’s intent to exclude non-essential processes.  
3. Establish a governance process to determine, on a case-by-case basis, which software processes qualify for PGE integration, ensuring both requirements reflect the same decision criteria.

Original_FR: A PGE ... The SDST shall integrate subsets of these processes into sets of PGEs that execute in DAAC centers.
Conflicting_FR: All PGEs shall execute within a unified ECS environment instead of separate DAAC centers.
Resolution:
1. Reconcile terminology by clarifying whether “DAAC centers” in Requirement A refer to physical locations or logical execution environments within the unified ECS framework, and update the requirement accordingly.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that PGE integration occurs logically within the unified ECS environment, even if data or services are associated with DAAC-designated functions.  
3. Revise Requirement B to allow for distributed execution within the unified ECS architecture, explicitly permitting PGE subsets to run in DAAC-associated nodes as long as they conform to ECS standards and interoperability protocols.

Original_FR: A PGE is a script which executes one or more MODIS software processes within the ECS environment.  A PGE shall not set or redefine environmental variables.
Conflicting_FR: A PGE executed by a PROCESS_ADMIN may set or redefine environment variables only during its first execution; subsequent executions by any PROCESS_ADMIN shall be prohibited from changing environment variables.
Resolution:
1. Align Requirement B with Requirement A by removing the exception that allows environment variable modification during the first execution, ensuring PGEs never set or redefine environment variables under any circumstances.  
2. If environment variable initialization is essential for correct PGE operation, refactor the system so that a trusted setup component (not the PGE itself) configures required environment variables before PGE execution, preserving the PGE’s restriction.  
3. Clarify and distinguish between “setting” (initial assignment) and “redefining” (modifying existing values) in both requirements, and explicitly permit only safe, one-time initialization by the PGE while prohibiting redefinition—provided this aligns with ECS security and stability policies.

Original_FR: A Program Administrator/Nursing Staff Member shall be able to add a new nursing student to the system.
Conflicting_FR: Only the System Administrator shall be permitted to add new nursing students.
Resolution:
1. Clarify roles and permissions by defining that only System Administrators can create new student accounts, while Program Administrators/Nursing Staff can initiate or request student additions (e.g., via a workflow or approval queue) that must be confirmed by a System Administrator.  
2. Reconcile the requirements by updating Requirement B to allow both System Administrators and authorized Program Administrators/Nursing Staff to add nursing students, provided appropriate role-based access controls and audit logging are implemented.  
3. Conduct stakeholder workshops to determine the true business need—whether delegation of student onboarding is essential—and revise the requirements accordingly to reflect agreed-upon responsibilities and security constraints.

Original_FR: A Program of Study shall consist of a program name and listing of required classes.
Conflicting_FR: A Program of Study may omit required classes and include elective components only.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "Program of Study" to distinguish between core programs (which must list required classes) and flexible or elective-only tracks, allowing both types under a unified framework.  
2. Revise Requirement A to state that a Program of Study shall include either a listing of required classes, elective components, or a combination of both, thereby accommodating Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a classification system (e.g., “Standard” vs. “Flexible” Programs of Study) where each type has its own set of rules, ensuring both requirements apply to their respective categories without contradiction.

Original_FR: A search described in requirement UCAR602 results in a displayed set of topics.
Conflicting_FR: The search described in UCAR602 shall output a summary report instead of displaying topics.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended user workflow in UCAR602 by consulting stakeholders to determine whether the primary output should be a topic list, a summary report, or both under different conditions.  
2. Modify the requirements to support context-dependent outputs—for example, display topics during interactive search and generate a summary report when explicitly requested or in batch mode.  
3. Update Requirement A or B to align with a single, consistent behavior (e.g., replace “displayed set of topics” with “summary report” in Requirement A if the report is the agreed-upon output).

Original_FR: A search described in requirement UCAR602 results in a displayed set of topics.
Conflicting_FR: A search described in requirement UCAR602 shall output a summary report instead of displaying topics.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intent of UCAR602 by consulting stakeholders to determine whether the primary output of the search should be a set of topics or a summary report, and update both Requirement A and Requirement B accordingly to reflect the agreed outcome.  
2. Define distinct contexts or user roles under which each output is appropriate (e.g., display topics for researchers, generate a summary report for administrators), and refine the requirements to specify these conditions explicitly.  
3. Modify the system behavior to support both outputs—display topics by default and provide an option to generate a summary report—then revise the requirements to reflect this dual-output capability without mutual exclusivity.

Original_FR: A search described in requirement UCAR702 results in a displayed set of topics.
Conflicting_FR: The search described in UCAR702 shall return topic identifiers only, not full topic details.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to specify that the displayed set of topics shows only topic identifiers (e.g., titles or IDs), aligning with Requirement B’s restriction against returning full topic details.  
2. Modify Requirement B to allow minimal topic metadata (e.g., title and ID) for display purposes, while still excluding comprehensive topic details, ensuring both usability and data limitation goals are met.  
3. Introduce a two-step search process: the initial search returns only identifiers (per Requirement B), and a subsequent user action (e.g., selection) triggers retrieval of full topic details, satisfying both requirements without direct conflict.

Original_FR: A search described in requirement UCAR702 results in a displayed set of topics.
Conflicting_FR: A search described in requirement UCAR702 shall return only topic identifiers, not full topic details.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that the "displayed set of topics" in Requirement A refers only to topic identifiers (e.g., titles or IDs), aligning with Requirement B’s restriction against returning full topic details.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that initial search results show only identifiers, with an option to retrieve full topic details upon user selection, satisfying both display and data minimization needs.  
3. Update both requirements to explicitly distinguish between “search result presentation” (identifiers only) and “topic detail retrieval” (full content on demand), ensuring consistency and traceability to UCAR702.

Original_FR: A search described in requirements UCAR201 and UCAR202 results in a displayed set of buildings that match the search input.
Conflicting_FR: The search described in UCAR201 and UCAR202 shall return only building IDs, not the full building list.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intent of "displayed set of buildings" in Requirement A—specify whether it refers to showing building IDs only or full building details—and align this definition with Requirement B.  
2. Modify Requirement A to state that the search results display building IDs initially, with an option to retrieve full building details upon user selection, satisfying both display and data minimization needs.  
3. Reconcile the requirements by defining two distinct outputs: a minimal result set (IDs only) per Requirement B for system-to-system communication, and a user-facing display (per Requirement A) that may include additional building information derived from those IDs.

Original_FR: A search described in requirements UCAR201 and UCAR202 results in a displayed set of buildings that match the search input.
Conflicting_FR: A search described in requirements UCAR201 and UCAR202 shall return only building IDs, not the full building list.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intent of both requirements with stakeholders: determine whether the user interface should display full building details (Requirement A) or if only IDs are to be returned by the backend (Requirement B), allowing for a layered architecture where the UI fetches details separately using the IDs.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that the displayed set of buildings is derived from the returned building IDs, ensuring consistency—i.e., the search returns only IDs (per Requirement B), and the system subsequently retrieves and displays full building information for those IDs.  
3. Reconcile terminology by distinguishing between “return” (system output/API response) and “display” (user-facing presentation), updating both requirements to reflect this distinction and eliminate ambiguity.

Original_FR: A search described in requirements UCAR203 results in a displayed set of POI that match the search input.
Conflicting_FR: If the quick search option is selected, the system shall use only the POI name field from the user input, ignore all other search parameters, and return the top 10 most frequently accessed POIs.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement A applies to standard (non-quick) searches, while Requirement B defines the behavior specifically for the quick search option—ensuring both can coexist by scoping each to its respective search mode.  
2. Modify Requirement A to explicitly exclude quick search scenarios, or add a clause stating that it is overridden by Requirement B when the quick search option is active.  
3. Update system documentation and user interface labels to clearly distinguish between standard and quick search functionalities, preventing user confusion about which behavior applies when.

Original_FR: A search described in requirements UCAR203 results in a displayed set of POIs that match the search input.
Conflicting_FR: The search described in UCAR203 shall exclude POIs from restricted areas.
Resolution:
1. Clarify in UCAR203 that the search results must include only POIs matching the search input *and* not located in restricted areas, thereby aligning both requirements under a unified definition of “matching.”  
2. Define “restricted areas” explicitly (e.g., via geofencing or access-control rules) and ensure the search logic filters out such POIs before displaying results, making exclusion part of the matching criteria.  
3. Add a traceability note to both requirements stating that Requirement B refines Requirement A by specifying an additional constraint on what constitutes a valid match.

Original_FR: A search described in requirements UCAR406 and UCAR407 results in a displayed set of events that match the search input.
Conflicting_FR: The search described in UCAR406 and UCAR407 shall only return future events, not past events.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to explicitly state that the displayed set of events is limited to future events, aligning it with Requirement B’s constraint.  
2. Update the system design to filter out past events during the search execution phase referenced in UCAR406 and UCAR407, ensuring both requirements are satisfied.  
3. Add a note or modifier to Requirement A specifying that “matching events” are subject to temporal constraints defined elsewhere (e.g., in Requirement B), avoiding redundancy while maintaining consistency.

Original_FR: A search described in requirements UCAR501 and UCAR502 results in a displayed set of employees that match the search input.
Conflicting_FR: Only HR administrators shall be able to perform searches on employees; regular users shall not access this function.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that the search functionality described in UCAR501 and UCAR502 is exclusively available to HR administrators, aligning Requirement A with the access restriction in Requirement B.  
2. Modify Requirement A to explicitly state that the displayed set of employees is only shown to authorized users (i.e., HR administrators), ensuring no ambiguity about user roles.  
3. Implement role-based access control so that the search interface and results are hidden or disabled for regular users, technically enforcing Requirement B while preserving the behavior in Requirement A for authorized users.

Original_FR: A single adult shall be able to lift and carry the UAV.
Conflicting_FR: The UAV shall require at least two adults to lift and carry it safely.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the context of use: Determine whether the UAV is intended for solo field operations (favoring Requirement A) or for controlled environments where team handling is feasible (favoring Requirement B), and align requirements accordingly.  
2. Define weight and safety thresholds: Establish a maximum weight limit that one adult can safely lift (e.g., per ergonomic guidelines); if the UAV exceeds this, revise Requirement A to allow optional two-person handling or add safety conditions.  
3. Introduce conditional phrasing: Modify the requirements to state that “a single adult shall be able to lift and carry the UAV under normal operating conditions, but tasks involving extended transport or hazardous terrain shall require two adults,” reconciling both safety and usability needs.

Original_FR: A single currency cannot occupy more than one slot.
Conflicting_FR: The CEP card may allow a currency to be assigned to multiple slots for redundancy.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of Requirement A by specifying that it applies only to standard (non-redundant) configurations, while allowing exceptions for redundancy as defined in Requirement B.  
2. Introduce a system mode distinction (e.g., "normal" vs. "redundant") where Requirement A holds in normal mode and Requirement B is permitted only in redundant mode under controlled conditions.  
3. Refine both requirements to explicitly state that multiple slot assignments for a currency are allowed only when explicitly enabled for redundancy purposes via the CEP card, ensuring mutual consistency.

Original_FR: A staff member shall have the ability to manage the progress of a part-time student.
Conflicting_FR: Only academic advisors shall be permitted to modify a part-time student’s class sequence.
Resolution:
1. Refine Requirement A to clarify that “manage the progress” excludes modifying the class sequence, which remains exclusive to academic advisors.  
2. Define “staff member” in Requirement A to explicitly exclude roles other than academic advisors when it comes to modifying class sequences.  
3. Introduce a role-based access control rule that allows staff members to manage non-curricular aspects of student progress (e.g., attendance, deadlines) while reserving class sequence modifications strictly for academic advisors.

Original_FR: A suitably authorized CPN user (e.g. the system administrator) shall be able to install and activate NGN managed services without the intervention of the subscribing CPN user.
Conflicting_FR: New NGN managed services shall be considered approved automatically if the subscribing CPN user does not respond within 3 seconds of the one-time code being issued.
Resolution:
1. Clarify roles and precedence: Define that the system administrator’s authority supersedes automatic approval timers, so Requirement A takes priority when a suitably authorized CPN user initiates installation.  
2. Adjust timing logic: Modify Requirement B to exclude installations initiated by authorized administrators from the 3-second auto-approval rule, ensuring manual administrative actions aren’t overridden.  
3. Introduce explicit consent flags: Require an explicit flag in the service activation request indicating whether it originates from an authorized administrator; if so, bypass the auto-approval mechanism entirely.

Original_FR: A suitably authorized CPN user (e.g., the system administrator) shall be able to install and activate NGN managed services.
Conflicting_FR: Only the service provider’s technical support personnel shall be allowed to install and activate NGN managed services.
Resolution:
1. Restrict CPN user installation rights to only those explicitly designated by the service provider (e.g., via a shared authorization registry), ensuring alignment with Requirement B.  
2. Redefine “suitably authorized CPN user” in Requirement A to mean users who are also credentialed members of the service provider’s technical support team, thereby harmonizing both requirements.  
3. Implement a dual-approval workflow where a CPN user can initiate installation, but final activation requires explicit confirmation from the service provider’s technical support personnel.

Original_FR: A suitably authorized CPN user shall be able to manage and configure provisioning capabilities.
Conflicting_FR: Only the NGN central management system shall manage provisioning; CPN users shall not have configuration rights.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "provisioning capabilities" in Requirement A to exclude core provisioning functions reserved for the NGN central management system, allowing CPN users only limited, delegated configuration rights (e.g., user-specific or tenant-level settings).  
2. Introduce role-based access control (RBAC) that distinguishes between "management" (reserved for NGN) and "configuration" (allowed for authorized CPN users under strict policy constraints), ensuring alignment with both requirements.  
3. Revise Requirement B to permit CPN user configuration under audit and approval workflows governed by the NGN central management system, maintaining centralized oversight while enabling necessary local flexibility.

Original_FR: A temperature setting value that is received from an individual thermostat shall be compared to the valid temperature range values.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall not perform validation against temperature range values to reduce processing overhead.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of validation: Perform temperature range validation only at the thermostat input interface (edge) and bypass redundant checks in downstream processing to minimize overhead.  
2. Introduce a configurable validation mode: Allow system operators to enable or disable temperature range validation based on operational priorities (safety vs. performance).  
3. Optimize validation logic: Implement an efficient, low-overhead validation mechanism (e.g., using precomputed lookup tables or bitmask checks) that satisfies both safety and performance concerns.

Original_FR: A transaction that passes merchant acquirer validations and is forwarded to the card issuer is paid for by the card issuer.
Conflicting_FR: The acquirer shall make payment to the merchant directly without card issuer validation.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the payment flow by specifying that the acquirer may provisionally settle with the merchant before issuer validation, but final liability rests with the card issuer only after successful validation.  
2. Revise Requirement B to state that the acquirer makes provisional or conditional payment to the merchant, subject to subsequent card issuer validation and potential reversal if validation fails.  
3. Define distinct transaction types or scenarios (e.g., low-risk vs. high-risk) where either Requirement A or B applies, ensuring mutual exclusivity based on risk thresholds or transaction attributes.

Original_FR: A user can select the desired product from the results and proceed to the product details.
Conflicting_FR: Only registered users shall be allowed to access product details; guest users shall not.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to clarify that only registered users can proceed to product details, aligning it with Requirement B (e.g., “A registered user can select the desired product from the results and proceed to the product details.”).  
2. Implement a conditional workflow where guest users can view limited product information (e.g., name, image, price) but must register or log in to access full product details.  
3. Add a system prompt that redirects unauthenticated users to a registration/login screen when they attempt to access product details, satisfying both requirements by allowing selection but restricting access based on authentication status.

Original_FR: A user can then select the product and view the details.
Conflicting_FR: Product detail viewing shall be available to standard users for a single 30-second preview per day; after the preview expires, standard users are blocked for the remainder of the day.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to specify that "viewing product details" is subject to user role and access limitations, aligning it with the constraints in Requirement B.  
2. Modify Requirement B to allow standard users to initiate a preview each time they select a product (up to a daily limit), ensuring the 30-second preview aligns with the user action described in Requirement A.  
3. Introduce a tiered access model where Requirement A applies fully to premium users, while standard users are governed by the preview restriction in Requirement B, and document this distinction explicitly in both requirements.

Original_FR: A user can then select the product and view the details.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall automatically display product details without requiring explicit user selection.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the user context: Define distinct scenarios (e.g., browsing vs. search results) where either explicit selection (Requirement A) or automatic display (Requirement B) applies, ensuring both behaviors are valid in their respective contexts.  
2. Implement a hybrid approach: Automatically show basic product details on hover or focus (supporting Requirement B), but require explicit selection to view full details (satisfying Requirement A).  
3. Reconcile through user preference: Allow users to choose their preferred interaction mode (automatic or manual) via a settings option, accommodating both requirements based on individual user needs.

Original_FR: A user may reorder routes or remove flight plans assigned to a UAV.
Conflicting_FR: Only flight controllers shall have permission to reorder or remove UAV flight plans.
Resolution:
1. Restrict the user capability in Requirement A to only those users who are authorized flight controllers, aligning it with the permission model in Requirement B.  
2. Clarify “user” in Requirement A by explicitly defining roles (e.g., “flight controller” vs. “operator”) and specify that only flight controllers may reorder or remove flight plans.  
3. Introduce a system-level access control mechanism that enforces role-based permissions, ensuring that the action described in Requirement A is only available to users with the flight controller role as stated in Requirement B.

Original_FR: A user must be logged on to be able to view or edit any medical information.
Conflicting_FR: Users shall be able to view general medical summaries without logging in.
Resolution:
1. Refine Requirement A to specify that only *protected* or *personalized* medical information requires authentication, while allowing public or anonymized general medical summaries to be accessible without login.  
2. Clarify in Requirement B that “general medical summaries” refer exclusively to non-sensitive, publicly available content (e.g., disease overviews, treatment guidelines) that does not contain any patient-specific data.  
3. Introduce a role-based access control model where unauthenticated users have read-only access to a predefined set of general medical content, while authenticated users gain access to personalized or restricted medical information.

Original_FR: Accepted input for price and destination search DESC: Integers should be accepted as input when a user searches by price or destination. If the system receives an invalid input the user should be informed and prompted to insert an accepted input.
Conflicting_FR: If the price and destination search is initiated, the THEMAS system shall accept only alphanumeric strings as input and shall reject all numerical entries, returning an error code to the calling service.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the input types for each search field: allow integers for the price field (as numeric values are logical for prices) and alphanumeric strings for the destination field, aligning both requirements with their respective contexts.  
2. Update Requirement B to distinguish between price and destination inputs—accept integers for price and alphanumeric strings for destination—removing the blanket rejection of all numerical entries.  
3. Define a unified input validation policy that specifies acceptable data types per parameter (e.g., integer for price, string for destination) and ensure both requirements reference this policy to avoid ambiguity.

Original_FR: Accepted input for price and destination search: Integers should be accepted.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall accept both integers and floating-point numbers for price and destination searches.
Resolution:
1. Update Requirement A to explicitly state that both integers and floating-point numbers are accepted, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Clarify in the system specification that integers are a subset of acceptable numeric inputs, and floating-point numbers are also supported, ensuring both requirements are consistently interpreted.  
3. Consolidate the two requirements into a single, unambiguous statement: “The system shall accept numeric inputs (both integers and floating-point numbers) for price and destination searches.”

Original_FR: Add a new dish — Given the administrator is logged in, When the administrator creates a new dish, Then the new dish should be added.
Conflicting_FR: Only the restaurant owner shall be allowed to add a new dish, not the administrator.
Resolution:
1. Clarify and align roles: Reconcile the definitions of "administrator" and "restaurant owner" in the system—either treat the restaurant owner as the only administrator with dish-creation privileges or explicitly restrict dish creation to the owner role, removing that capability from other administrators.  
2. Update Requirement A to specify that only the restaurant owner (a specific type of administrator) can add a new dish, thereby making it consistent with Requirement B.  
3. Implement role-based access control (RBAC) where the “add dish” function is granted exclusively to the restaurant owner role, ensuring technical enforcement aligns with both requirements once clarified.

Original_FR: Add a new restaurant owner — Given the administrator is logged in...
Conflicting_FR: Only the system super-administrator shall be allowed to create new restaurant owners.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that "administrator" in Requirement A specifically refers to the "system super-administrator" to align with Requirement B’s stricter permission scope.  
2. Revise Requirement A to explicitly state that only the system super-administrator can perform the action, removing ambiguity about administrator roles.  
3. Introduce role-based access control definitions in the requirements glossary to distinguish between general administrators and super-administrators, ensuring consistent interpretation across all requirements.

Original_FR: Add a new restaurant owner Given the administrator is logged in When the administrator creates a new restaurant owner Then the new restaurant owner should be added
Conflicting_FR: Creation of new restaurant owners shall be allowed for SUPER_ADMIN only during a single 60-second annual provisioning window; outside that 60-second window no creations are permitted.
Resolution:
1. Update Requirement A to specify that only a SUPER_ADMIN (not just any administrator) can create a new restaurant owner, aligning it with the stricter role constraint in Requirement B.  
2. Modify Requirement A to include the temporal constraint from Requirement B, clarifying that creation is permitted only during the designated 60-second annual provisioning window.  
3. If business needs justify broader or more frequent creation capabilities, revise Requirement B to relax either the role restriction (e.g., allow other admin roles) or the time window (e.g., extend frequency or duration), and document the rationale for the change.

Original_FR: Add a new restaurant type — Given the administrator is logged in...
Conflicting_FR: Restaurant owners shall be allowed to define new restaurant types independently.
Resolution:
1. Restrict the creation of new restaurant types to administrators only, and remove the ability for restaurant owners to define new types independently.  
2. Allow restaurant owners to propose new restaurant types, but require administrator approval before they become active in the system.  
3. Enable both roles to create new restaurant types, but maintain separate namespaces or scopes (e.g., global types by admins, private/custom types by owners) to avoid overlap and inconsistency.

Original_FR: Add restaurant information — Given the administrator is logged in...
Conflicting_FR: Only the restaurant owner shall be permitted to add or modify restaurant information.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that "administrator" in Requirement A refers specifically to the restaurant owner, ensuring role definitions align with Requirement B.  
2. Restrict the "add restaurant information" functionality to restaurant owners only, removing general administrator privileges for this action unless they are also the owner.  
3. Introduce a role-based access control system where administrators can only add/modify restaurant information if explicitly granted permission by the restaurant owner or through an ownership verification process.

Original_FR: Adding information with mandatory and optional fields — Given the restaurant owner has filled in the mandatory fields...
Conflicting_FR: The system shall reject submissions containing optional fields until verified by an administrator.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that optional fields may be filled in by the restaurant owner but are not processed or displayed until administrator verification, allowing submission to proceed with only mandatory fields validated initially.  
2. Modify Requirement B to apply only to specific sensitive optional fields (e.g., promotional claims), while permitting other optional fields to be submitted and stored without verification.  
3. Introduce a two-stage submission process: the first stage accepts and saves all fields (mandatory and optional) as a draft, and the second stage—after administrator verification—activates the optional field data for public use.

Original_FR: Adding information with mandatory fields — Given the restaurant owner has filled in the mandatory fields...
Conflicting_FR: Only administrators shall be permitted to add new restaurant information.
Resolution:
1. Clarify roles by specifying that only administrators can initiate the addition of new restaurant information, but allow restaurant owners to fill in mandatory fields within a workflow supervised or approved by an administrator.  
2. Revise Requirement A to apply only to updating or completing drafts of restaurant information that have already been initiated by an administrator, ensuring Requirement B’s access control remains intact.  
3. Introduce a two-step process where restaurant owners submit draft information (including mandatory fields), and administrators formally add the information to the system after review, satisfying both data completeness and permission constraints.

Original_FR: Adding information with mandatory fields Given the restaurant owner has filled in the mandatory fields of the form When the restaurant owner submits the form Then the information about the restaurant should be added
Conflicting_FR: New restaurant submissions shall be auto-approved if the regional manager does not review them within 2 seconds of submission; no human approval is then required.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the approval workflow by specifying that auto-approval (Requirement B) only applies after successful validation of mandatory fields (Requirement A), ensuring data integrity before automatic processing.  
2. Adjust the auto-approval time threshold in Requirement B from “2 seconds” to a more realistic duration (e.g., 24–48 hours) to allow reasonable human review while still supporting timely onboarding.  
3. Introduce a system state where restaurant data is stored as “pending” upon form submission (satisfying Requirement A’s storage intent) and only becomes publicly visible or active upon either manual approval or auto-approval per Requirement B.

Original_FR: after every user request, new maps are needed to be loaded.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall cache maps and reuse them instead of loading new ones after every request.
Resolution:
1. Refine Requirement A to specify that new maps are only loaded when the requested map data has changed or is not already cached, aligning with caching behavior.  
2. Modify Requirement B to define cache invalidation rules (e.g., time-based or event-based) so that "new maps" in Requirement A are interpreted as "fresh or updated maps," which may come from cache if still valid.  
3. Introduce a conditional clause in both requirements: load new maps only when necessary (e.g., user location or query parameters change significantly), otherwise reuse cached maps.

Original_FR: After installing the Application from iTunes or the App store the user will be asked to login.
Conflicting_FR: Application launch without login shall be allowed only once per device ever; all subsequent launches shall require full authentication.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that the initial post-install launch counts as the one allowed "login-free" launch, so the user is not prompted to log in immediately after installation but only on the second and subsequent launches.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that login is requested only after the first (login-free) launch, aligning it with Requirement B’s “once per device ever” rule.  
3. Implement a device-bound flag that tracks whether the app has been launched before; use this flag to enforce Requirement B while ensuring Requirement A applies only from the second launch onward.

Original_FR: After installing the Application from iTunes or the App Store, the user will be asked to login.
Conflicting_FR: The user shall be automatically logged in after installation without a manual login step.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended user experience with stakeholders: Determine whether the product should prioritize security (requiring explicit login) or convenience (enabling automatic login), and align both requirements accordingly.  
2. Implement context-aware login behavior: Automatically log in the user only if they are already authenticated on the device (e.g., via Apple ID or a trusted session); otherwise, prompt for manual login.  
3. Revise Requirement A to exclude first-time installations where automatic login is supported, or revise Requirement B to apply only under specific conditions (e.g., reinstallation or enterprise deployment).

Original_FR: After selecting one of the months, the system shall generate the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall generate a PDF report instead of an ASCII report file.
Resolution:
1. Clarify stakeholder priorities by determining whether ASCII or PDF is the required output format, and update one requirement to align with the agreed standard.  
2. Modify Requirement A to allow for configurable report formats (e.g., “The system shall generate a report file in the user-selected format, which may include ASCII or PDF”), thereby accommodating both needs.  
3. If both formats are needed in different contexts, specify conditions under which each format is generated (e.g., ASCII for system-to-system interfaces, PDF for user-facing reports) and revise both requirements accordingly.

Original_FR: After selecting one of the months, the system shall generate the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: After selecting one of the months, the system shall generate a PDF report file.
Resolution:
1. Modify the requirements to specify that the system shall generate both ASCII and PDF report files upon month selection, ensuring compatibility with different user needs.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable option allowing the user to choose the desired output format (ASCII or PDF) after selecting a month.  
3. Clarify the intended use cases for each format and assign them to distinct user roles or contexts (e.g., ASCII for system logs, PDF for end-user reports), then update the requirements accordingly.

Original_FR: Agenda is restricted to what is available in the feeds on the list of agenda RSS-feeds.
Conflicting_FR: Agenda items may include externally sourced content beyond the RSS feeds.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope by defining a primary source (RSS feeds) for agenda items while allowing exceptions for externally sourced content under specific, documented conditions (e.g., editorial override or user-submitted events).  
2. Introduce a tiered agenda structure: core agenda items derived strictly from RSS feeds, and an optional supplementary section that may include externally sourced content.  
3. Update Requirement A to permit external content as long as it is explicitly flagged or tagged as non-RSS-derived, ensuring transparency and traceability of sources.

Original_FR: All information must be updatable on the website.
Conflicting_FR: Only administrators shall be able to update website information; general users shall not.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to specify that "All information must be updatable by authorized users on the website," aligning it with the access control in Requirement B.  
2. Define user roles explicitly in the requirements, stating that administrators have update privileges while general users have read-only access, ensuring both completeness and security.  
3. Implement a permissions-based system where updatable content is conditionally accessible based on user role, satisfying both the need for updatable information and restricted access.

Original_FR: All information must be viewable on the website.
Conflicting_FR: Certain sensitive information shall not be viewable on the website.
Resolution:
1. Refine Requirement A to exclude sensitive information by specifying that "All non-sensitive information must be viewable on the website."  
2. Define clear criteria or categories for what constitutes "sensitive information" in Requirement B, and ensure these are formally documented and agreed upon by stakeholders.  
3. Implement role-based access control so that sensitive information is viewable only to authorized users, thereby satisfying both requirements through conditional visibility.

Original_FR: All letter requests must be formatted according to guidelines specified by the Print Letter Utility system.
Conflicting_FR: Letter requests may follow either the system format or user-defined templates.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that user-defined templates must still comply with the core formatting guidelines of the Print Letter Utility system, ensuring consistency while allowing customization within defined boundaries.  
2. Introduce a validation step where user-defined templates are automatically checked against the Print Letter Utility’s mandatory formatting rules before submission.  
3. Revise Requirement A to specify only mandatory formatting elements (e.g., headers, fonts, margins), while allowing optional stylistic variations via user-defined templates as permitted by Requirement B.

Original_FR: All on-line messages must have the ability to include card issuer discretionary data from the CEP card.
Conflicting_FR: Online messages shall exclude issuer discretionary data for security reasons.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "issuer discretionary data" by defining which specific data elements are permitted (e.g., non-sensitive fields) versus prohibited (e.g., security-sensitive fields), allowing Requirement A to support limited inclusion while honoring Requirement B’s security intent.  
2. Implement conditional inclusion: allow issuer discretionary data in online messages only when transmitted over secure, authenticated channels and masked or encrypted as needed to satisfy security constraints.  
3. Reconcile stakeholder needs by engaging both business (e.g., payment processing) and security teams to jointly define acceptable use cases, potentially resulting in a refined requirement that permits controlled, audited inclusion of discretionary data.

Original_FR: All Pilot communication with the UAV shall require secure connections.
Conflicting_FR: Pilots may use unsecured connections for training flights.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement B to specify that unsecured connections are only permitted in isolated, non-operational training environments with no access to live systems or sensitive data.  
2. Introduce a conditional clause in Requirement A to exempt approved training scenarios, ensuring such exemptions are formally documented and controlled.  
3. Implement a secure-but-simplified communication protocol for training flights that meets baseline security standards while accommodating training usability needs.

Original_FR: All registered customer information shall be stored and maintained in the database even after account cancellation.
Conflicting_FR: Customer data shall be permanently deleted once the account is canceled.
Resolution:
1. Implement a data anonymization or pseudonymization process upon account cancellation, retaining non-identifiable records for business analytics while complying with data deletion obligations.  
2. Introduce a configurable retention policy that allows customers to choose between immediate deletion or archival (e.g., for legal or audit purposes), aligning with applicable data protection regulations.  
3. Clarify the scope of “customer data” in each requirement—distinguish between personally identifiable information (to be deleted) and transactional or aggregated data (to be retained)—and update both requirements accordingly.

Original_FR: All searchable product-specific metadata in MODIS standard products shall be formatted as specified in DID 311.
Conflicting_FR: MODIS metadata may follow updated format guidelines replacing DID 311.
Resolution:
1. Update Requirement A to reference the current governing document (e.g., “as specified in the latest version of DID 311 or its successor”) to accommodate future revisions.  
2. Clarify in Requirement B that any updated format guidelines must be formally approved and backward-compatible with existing systems relying on DID 311.  
3. Establish a change control process that mandates synchronized updates to both requirements whenever DID 311 is superseded, ensuring consistency.

Original_FR: All streaming movie sales will be logged in the database and accessible by the administrator.
Conflicting_FR: Sales logs shall be accessible to both administrators and authorized auditors.
Resolution:
1. Update Requirement A to explicitly include authorized auditors as users who can access the sales logs, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Define clear access control roles in the system so that both administrators and authorized auditors have appropriate, role-based access to the logged sales data.  
3. Clarify the term “accessible” in both requirements to specify whether access includes read-only permissions, export capabilities, or audit trails, ensuring consistency in scope and intent.

Original_FR: All streaming movie sales will be logged in the database and accessible by the administrator.
Conflicting_FR: Streaming movie sales logs shall be viewable by admins for a single continuous 10-second interval each month; for all other times only internal audit may view them.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to specify that administrator access is limited to the 10-second monthly window defined in Requirement B, aligning both requirements on access scope.  
2. Introduce role-based access control distinguishing between “administrator” and “internal audit,” ensuring only internal audit has continuous access while administrators are restricted to the 10-second interval.  
3. Revise Requirement B to allow administrators full access but implement strict logging and alerting for such access, satisfying auditability while preserving administrative oversight.

Original_FR: All UAV data transmission shall be secure against unauthorized access.
Conflicting_FR: UAV data may be transmitted in plaintext during test operations.
Resolution:
1. Restrict plaintext transmission in Requirement B to isolated, non-production test environments with no external connectivity and document this as a controlled exception to Requirement A.  
2. Update Requirement A to explicitly allow temporary deviations for authorized test operations under defined security controls (e.g., physical isolation, access logs).  
3. Require encryption even during testing but permit the use of test-specific keys or simplified cryptographic protocols that balance security with testability.

Original_FR: All UAV data transmission shall be secure from unauthorized access.
Conflicting_FR: UAV data may be broadcast without encryption for telemetry testing.
Resolution:
1. Restrict unencrypted telemetry broadcasts to isolated, secure test environments with no external network access to prevent unauthorized exposure.  
2. Implement a configurable security mode that allows encryption to be temporarily disabled only during authorized testing sessions, with automatic re-enabling afterward.  
3. Require explicit operator authentication and logging whenever unencrypted data transmission is activated, ensuring accountability and traceability.

Original_FR: All users shall be able to zoom into images using standard gestures.
Conflicting_FR: Only premium users shall be able to zoom into images.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement A to specify that basic zoom functionality is available to all users, while enhanced or advanced zoom features (e.g., higher magnification levels, lossless zoom) are reserved for premium users.  
2. Clarify user roles and permissions in the requirements: define “standard gestures” as a baseline interaction available to all, but restrict actual zoom capability based on subscription status, ensuring UI feedback informs non-premium users of the premium-only feature.  
3. Merge the requirements into a single conditional statement: “All users shall be able to attempt zoom gestures, but image zoom functionality shall only be enabled for premium users.”

Original_FR: All users should be able to go back to the product category page via the back button.
Conflicting_FR: Only registered users shall have access to the back navigation feature.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement A to specify that the back button functionality is available only to registered users, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Implement a conditional back navigation feature: allow all users to navigate back via browser controls or breadcrumbs, but restrict any custom “back” button (e.g., in-app UI) to registered users only.  
3. Clarify the intent behind Requirement B—if security or tracking is the concern—consider alternative solutions (e.g., logging unregistered user navigation without restricting access) to preserve universal usability while meeting the underlying need.

Original_FR: All users should be able to navigate using the sub-navigation icons to access PDFs, videos, and contacts.
Conflicting_FR: Only authenticated users shall access the catalogue PDF and 360 videos.
Resolution:
1. Restrict the sub-navigation icons for PDFs and 360 videos to only appear or be functional for authenticated users, ensuring navigation aligns with access control.  
2. Implement role-based visibility in the sub-navigation so that unauthenticated users see only publicly accessible content (e.g., general contacts), while authenticated users see all options.  
3. Modify Requirement A to clarify that navigation to restricted content (PDFs and videos) is conditional on authentication, preserving usability without violating security constraints.

Original_FR: All users should be able to search by keyword or product ID.
Conflicting_FR: Only employees shall be able to perform keyword or product ID searches.
Resolution:
1. Clarify user roles: Define "users" in Requirement A to exclude non-employees (e.g., specify "authenticated users" or "registered customers"), aligning it with Requirement B’s restriction to employees only.  
2. Differentiate search capabilities: Allow all users basic search functionality (e.g., by product name or category), while reserving advanced keyword or product ID searches exclusively for employees.  
3. Reconcile intent through stakeholder consultation: Engage stakeholders to determine whether broad search access is truly needed for all users or if Requirement A was mistakenly overgeneralized, then revise accordingly.

Original_FR: All Users will be able to view the next product by swiping left to right .
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall display the next product only after the user double-taps the product image.
Resolution:
1. Define distinct user roles or contexts: Allow swiping for general users while reserving double-tap navigation for specific user types (e.g., reviewers or admins) or modes (e.g., detailed review mode).
