2. Prioritize one interaction method and deprecate the other: Choose either swipe or double-tap as the primary navigation method based on usability testing or stakeholder priorities, and update requirements accordingly.  
3. Combine both interactions with clear precedence: Enable both gestures but specify that double-tap takes priority in contexts where both could apply (e.g., disable swipe during image zoom or annotation mode).

Original_FR: All users will be able to view the next product by swiping left to right.
Conflicting_FR: The next product shall be displayed automatically without requiring a swipe action.
Resolution:
1. Implement both navigation modes: allow automatic advancement to the next product by default, but also support manual swiping for user control—ensuring swiping doesn’t interfere with auto-advance (e.g., pause auto-advance during interaction).  
2. Make the behavior configurable: let users choose between automatic display or manual swiping via a settings option, satisfying both usability preferences.  
3. Clarify context of use: define specific scenarios where each requirement applies (e.g., auto-advance in kiosk mode, swipe navigation in interactive mode) and implement conditional logic accordingly.

Original_FR: Application should do the distance calculations and geolocation information for user/site and nearby sites.
Conflicting_FR: If the distance calculation feature is invoked, the system shall require the user to manually enter the latitude and longitude coordinates for both the origin and destination points.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement B to allow automatic geolocation (e.g., via GPS or IP-based location) as the default method, with manual coordinate entry as an optional fallback for users who prefer or require it.  
2. Clarify Requirement A to specify that geolocation information can be obtained either automatically or through user input, ensuring alignment with Requirement B’s manual entry condition only when automatic methods are unavailable or disabled.  
3. Introduce a system setting or user preference that lets the user choose between automatic geolocation and manual coordinate entry, satisfying both requirements based on context and user control.

Original_FR: At the start of each turn the product shall notify each player of his or her status.
Conflicting_FR: The Game Master account shall be permitted to view/announce player status only during the first 5 seconds after a turn starts; outside that window no viewing/announcing is allowed.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to exclude the Game Master from automatic status notifications, ensuring only regular players receive them at the start of each turn.  
2. Adjust Requirement B to allow the Game Master to view (but not announce) player status at any time, while restricting announcements to the first 5 seconds after a turn starts.  
3. Clarify that the automatic notification in Requirement A is sent directly to each player (not via the Game Master), so Requirement B’s restriction on the Game Master does not interfere with the system’s direct notifications.

Original_FR: Both IMS capable devices with or without UICC and non-IMS capable devices without UICC shall be supported.
Conflicting_FR: IMS service access shall be permitted to devices with an active UICC only for a single TCP session lasting no longer than 3 seconds; any longer sessions shall be terminated.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement B applies only to IMS-capable devices, thereby allowing non-IMS capable devices (per Requirement A) to operate without UICC restrictions.  
2. Revise Requirement B to exempt devices without UICC from the 3-second TCP session limit, ensuring alignment with Requirement A’s support for such devices.  
3. Introduce a conditional clause in Requirement B stating that the UICC and session duration constraints apply solely when IMS services are actively being used, not merely when the device is IMS-capable.

Original_FR: Build Ajax Response to update home page with a link to nearby site based on user location
Conflicting_FR: When the user location is not available, the system shall display a default list of popular sites and update the home page with the generated Ajax Response.
Resolution:
1. Design the Ajax response logic to include a conditional check: if user location is available, return nearby sites; otherwise, fall back to the default list of popular sites—ensuring both requirements are satisfied within a single unified response flow.  
2. Clarify and document that Requirement A implicitly assumes location availability, while Requirement B explicitly handles the absence of location data; align both by treating Requirement B as the fallback behavior for Requirement A.  
3. Implement a modular backend service that returns either location-based or default site data based on input parameters, allowing the frontend Ajax call to remain consistent regardless of location availability.

Original_FR: Build the search engine based on the search form and sites available
Conflicting_FR: When the search form is submitted, the system shall ignore any user-input keywords and instead execute a pre-defined search for "breaking news" across all available sites.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intent of Requirement A: If the search engine is meant to support user-driven queries, revise Requirement B to only apply under specific conditions (e.g., when a "breaking news" mode is activated), preserving general search functionality otherwise.  
2. Modify Requirement A to explicitly state that the search engine includes both user-initiated searches and system-defined searches (like "breaking news"), ensuring both behaviors are supported without contradiction.  
3. Replace the absolute language in Requirement B (“ignore any user-input keywords”) with conditional logic (e.g., “if no keywords are provided, default to ‘breaking news’”), aligning it with a flexible interpretation of Requirement A.

Original_FR: CNDs connected to the CPN and devoted to specific managed services shall be remotely manageable
Conflicting_FR: Full remote management privileges granted to CND-MGMT-ADMIN shall be limited to a single 30-second session per day; attempts outside that session are denied.
Resolution:
1. Refine Requirement B to allow continuous remote management for CNDs dedicated to specific managed services (as in Requirement A), while retaining the 30-second session limit only for non-service-specific CNDs.  
2. Introduce role-based access control: grant CND-MGMT-ADMIN extended or unlimited remote management sessions for service-dedicated CNDs, while keeping the 30-second restriction for other contexts.  
3. Modify Requirement A to specify that remote manageability includes scheduled or time-bound access, aligning with Requirement B’s session constraint without compromising operational needs.

Original_FR: CNG shall perform a link layer multicast to unicast translation if the STB or media gateway are connected using customer network technologies not supporting multicast flows (e.g. powerlines wireless).
Conflicting_FR: Users holding the ORES key shall gain full clinical access only for the first 10 seconds after login, after which all access is restricted.
Resolution:
1. Clarify scope separation: Confirm that Requirement A pertains to network infrastructure for STB/media gateway communication, while Requirement B governs clinical system access control—ensuring they apply to distinct subsystems with no functional overlap.  
2. Refactor requirement domains: Assign Requirement A to the networking/transport layer specifications and Requirement B to the clinical application security policy, preventing cross-domain interference through architectural boundaries.  
3. Validate stakeholder intent: Engage relevant stakeholders (e.g., network engineers vs. clinical system designers) to verify that the requirements address different user roles, systems, or contexts, thereby eliminating perceived conflict through precise contextual scoping.

Original_FR: Confirmed registration Given the restaurant owner has applied for verification And has not received a confirmation e-mail after registration When the restaurant owner receives a confirmation e-mail Then the restaurant owner should be able to log in
Conflicting_FR: Verified restaurant owner status shall be granted only once per device, and any subsequent login attempts require re-verification regardless of manual approval.
Resolution:
1. Align the verification scope by clarifying that "Verified restaurant owner status" in Requirement B refers to device-specific session tokens, not the overall account login eligibility granted by Requirement A.  
2. Modify Requirement B to allow continued logins from already-verified devices without re-verification, while still enforcing one-time initial verification per device as stated.  
3. Update Requirement A to specify that successful email confirmation grants account-level login rights, but access from each new device still requires a one-time device-specific verification as per Requirement B.

Original_FR: Consumers must be able to load electronic value to their cards at any participating load device regardless of the location provided that the currency that is requested is supported by the acquirer and the card issuer
Conflicting_FR: Card loading shall be permitted only for one transaction per hour per device, even for devices owned by the card issuer.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that the "one transaction per hour per device" limit in Requirement B applies per card (or per consumer), not per device globally, allowing multiple consumers to load value on the same device within an hour while still limiting individual usage.  
2. Introduce an exception in Requirement B for issuer-owned devices, permitting higher transaction frequency (e.g., no hourly limit) to align with Requirement A’s intent of universal accessibility when currency and acquirer support are present.  
3. Refine Requirement A to acknowledge operational constraints by adding that loading is subject to reasonable usage policies (such as those in Requirement B), ensuring both accessibility and system integrity.

Original_FR: Contact with the vendors and give permission to sell their product under the site after testing the product quality.
Conflicting_FR: Product selling permissions shall be granted only during a 5-second daily approval window; outside this window, vendors must wait.
Resolution:
1. Align the product quality testing process to conclude just before the 5-second daily approval window, ensuring vendors are ready for immediate permission granting during that window.  
2. Modify Requirement B to allow pre-approved vendors (whose products have already passed quality testing) to receive permissions outside the 5-second window via an automated system, while retaining the window for new or unverified vendors.  
3. Extend or make the approval window configurable (e.g., based on vendor tier or product category) to accommodate the operational need in Requirement A without compromising control objectives.

Original_FR: Each time an alarm is requested, an alarm event shall be recorded.
Conflicting_FR: If an alarm is requested for the first time, an alarm event shall be recorded.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of Requirement A by specifying that it applies only to unique or distinct alarm requests, aligning it with Requirement B’s intent to record only the first occurrence.  
2. Revise Requirement B to explicitly state that subsequent alarm requests do not generate additional alarm events, thereby resolving ambiguity with Requirement A.  
3. Introduce a new requirement that defines when duplicate alarm requests should or should not trigger event recording, and update both A and B to reference this rule consistently.

Original_FR: Each time an alarm is requested, an alarm event shall be recorded.
Conflicting_FR: The alarm event shall be recorded only once per unique alarm type, and within 2 seconds of the first request.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to specify that an alarm event is recorded only for the first request of each unique alarm type within a 2-second window, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Clarify in Requirement B that subsequent alarm requests of the same type within 2 seconds do not trigger new recordings, while ensuring Requirement A explicitly excludes duplicates.  
3. Introduce a system-level rule that de-duplicates alarm requests by type within a 2-second interval before applying Requirement A, ensuring both requirements are satisfied through coordinated interpretation.

Original_FR: Filling in menu field Given the restaurant owner wants to fill in the menu field in the form When the restaurant owner provides dish name And dish description And dish price Then the restaurant owner has filled in the menu field in the form
Conflicting_FR: When the restaurant owner provides dish name, dish price, and dish category, the system shall validate the price against the predefined category price range and populate the dish description automatically.
Resolution:
1. Align the input fields by updating Requirement A to include dish category as a mandatory field, ensuring consistency with Requirement B’s validation logic.  
2. Modify Requirement B to allow manual entry of dish description when provided, falling back to auto-population only if the description is left blank.  
3. Clarify in both requirements whether dish description is user-provided or system-generated, and define precedence rules (e.g., user input overrides auto-populated values).

Original_FR: Filling in optional fields Given the restaurant owner of a restaurant wants to fill in optional fields in the form When the restaurant owner provides restaurant description And menu And type of restaurant And picture of restaurant And mobile phone Then the restaurant owner has filled in optional fields in the form
Conflicting_FR: The restaurant owner shall only fill in mandatory fields in the form, and any optional fields provided shall be ignored by the system.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the business rules: Determine whether optional fields should be allowed and processed or strictly ignored, and update both requirements to reflect the agreed policy.  
2. Revise Requirement B to permit optional fields to be saved if provided, aligning it with Requirement A, while ensuring mandatory fields remain the minimum required input.  
3. If optional fields must be ignored for compliance or simplicity, remove or deactivate optional field inputs in the UI and update Requirement A to reflect that only mandatory fields are accepted.

Original_FR: Following a successful query the search feature will query the application database to display the search results. Following an unsuccessful query the search feature will display an error message that no matches were found
Conflicting_FR: Following an unsuccessful query, the search feature shall retry automatically until a match is found instead of displaying an error message.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "unsuccessful query": distinguish between transient failures (e.g., network issues) that warrant automatic retries and definitive no-match results that should trigger an error message.  
2. Modify Requirement B to limit automatic retries to specific error conditions (e.g., timeouts or service unavailability), while preserving Requirement A’s behavior for genuine no-match scenarios.  
3. Introduce a user-configurable setting or system policy that determines whether the system retries or displays an error after an unsuccessful query, allowing context-dependent behavior.

Original_FR: For data with enough structuring, web scraping will be used to programmatically extract all the data needed and store it in our database.
Conflicting_FR: Web scraping shall not be used for extracting any data; all data must be entered manually.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the data sources and categorize them: allow web scraping only for publicly available, structured data that complies with legal and ethical guidelines, while requiring manual entry for sensitive or unstructured data.  
2. Reconcile stakeholder priorities by evaluating the trade-offs—replace one requirement with a hybrid approach that permits limited, approved web scraping under strict governance, ensuring alignment with compliance and operational needs.  
3. Validate the origin of the conflict—if Requirement B stems from legal or policy constraints, update Requirement A to exclude prohibited sources; if it’s based on outdated assumptions, revise Requirement B to reflect current technical capabilities and safeguards.

Original_FR: For each class within a sequence for a cohort Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members shall be able to specify which quarter that class will be offered.
Conflicting_FR: Only Program Administrators shall be able to specify the quarter; Nursing Staff Members shall not have permission to define class quarters.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement A to explicitly restrict quarter assignment permissions to Program Administrators only, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. If Nursing Staff input is needed for scheduling, modify the system design to allow them to propose or recommend quarters (without final authority), while retaining exclusive approval rights for Program Administrators.  
3. Clarify role definitions and responsibilities in the requirements documentation to ensure consistent interpretation of "specify"—distinguishing between suggestion, approval, and final assignment actions.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall examine the defensive grid of the defensive player to determine whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not examine the defensive grid before determining whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the timing and scope of "examine" in Requirement B—allow examination of the defensive grid as part of the hit/miss determination process, but prohibit any prior or separate inspection unrelated to that specific shot evaluation.  
2. Revise Requirement B to state: “The product shall not examine the defensive grid for any purpose other than determining whether the current shot was a hit or miss,” aligning it with Requirement A.  
3. Merge both requirements into a single, unambiguous statement such as: “For each shot, the product shall examine the defensive grid solely and immediately to determine whether the shot was a hit or miss.”

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall mark the shot as a hit or miss on the offensive grid of the offensive player.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not display offensive shot results to maintain player privacy.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to store hit/miss results internally without displaying them on the offensive grid, satisfying privacy while preserving game logic.  
2. Revise Requirement B to allow limited visual feedback (e.g., abstract indicators like color changes) that convey shot outcome to the shooter without revealing sensitive information to opponents.  
3. Introduce a user-configurable privacy setting that lets players choose whether to display their own shot results, balancing functionality and privacy preferences.

Original_FR: For each shot the product shall notify the players whether the shot was a hit or miss.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not notify the players immediately; notifications will only be sent after the end of the turn.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to specify that hit/miss notifications are provided at the end of the turn rather than immediately after each shot, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Introduce a configurable setting allowing players to choose between immediate feedback (per Requirement A) or delayed feedback until turn end (per Requirement B), satisfying both user preferences and system constraints.  
3. Clarify the definition of “notify” in Requirement A to allow for internal logging or silent tracking of hits/misses during the turn, with visible player notifications only occurring at turn end as required by Requirement B.

Original_FR: For each turn the product shall allow the offensive player to define a shot.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall automatically define the offensive player's shot without user input.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the game mode context: Allow Requirement A to apply in manual/user-controlled modes and Requirement B in automated or AI-assisted modes, making the behavior mode-dependent.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable setting that lets players choose between manual shot definition (Requirement A) and automatic shot generation (Requirement B).  
3. Refine the requirements to specify that the system may suggest or auto-define a shot (Requirement B) but must allow the player to override or redefine it (Requirement A) before execution.

Original_FR: For each turn the product shall identify one player as offensive status and one player as defensive status.
Conflicting_FR: Both players shall be assigned offensive and defensive status simultaneously.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended gameplay model: Determine whether the game is turn-based with alternating roles (supporting Requirement A) or real-time/simultaneous action (supporting Requirement B), and revise one requirement to align with the chosen model.  
2. Refine terminology: Replace ambiguous terms like “simultaneously” in Requirement B with precise language—e.g., “within the same game phase” or “at the start of each turn”—to ensure compatibility with Requirement A’s turn-based structure.  
3. Introduce a hybrid role assignment: Allow both players to have dual roles (offensive and defensive) within the same turn, but designate primary/secondary roles to satisfy the “one offensive, one defensive” constraint while preserving simultaneous status assignment.

Original_FR: For totally unstructured information, AI libraries will be used to programmatically extract and store any information possible
Conflicting_FR: For totally unstructured information, manual extraction shall be used to ensure accuracy.
Resolution:
1. Define a hybrid approach: Use AI libraries for initial extraction of totally unstructured information, followed by human review and validation to ensure accuracy.  
2. Establish criteria-based routing: Automatically classify the complexity or sensitivity of unstructured data and route high-accuracy-critical items to manual extraction while using AI for lower-risk content.  
3. Clarify scope with stakeholder input: Engage stakeholders to determine which types of unstructured information require manual extraction versus where AI suffices, updating requirements accordingly.

Original_FR: Formula substitutionary ingredients shall be kept in persisted order
Conflicting_FR: Substitutionary ingredients may be reordered automatically by the system for optimization.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that the persisted order in Requirement A refers to the user-defined default order, while allowing Requirement B’s automatic reordering to apply only during runtime optimization without altering the persisted baseline.  
2. Introduce a configuration flag or policy setting that lets users choose between preserving strict persisted order (fulfilling Requirement A) or enabling system-driven reordering (fulfilling Requirement B), making the behavior explicit and controllable.  
3. Modify Requirement A to specify that the persisted order must be maintained unless explicitly overridden by an authorized optimization process, thereby reconciling persistence with conditional reordering.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the full text of the news articles, the first paragraphs are shown.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the source of content by specifying whether the system should extract from article titles or full text, and align both requirements to use the same source (e.g., use only full text for consistency).  
2. Define a unified display rule that combines both intents—for example, show the first line of the title followed by the first paragraph of the full text—ensuring both requirements are partially satisfied without contradiction.  
3. Prioritize one requirement based on user needs or business goals (e.g., favor full-text paragraphs for richer context) and formally deprecate or revise the other to avoid overlap.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, no preview lines shall be shown to minimize clutter.
Resolution:
1. Introduce a user-configurable setting that allows users to choose whether preview lines are shown or hidden, satisfying both preferences.  
2. Display preview lines only on user interaction (e.g., hover or tap), minimizing clutter by default while still providing access to the content.  
3. Clarify stakeholder priorities through a requirements workshop to determine whether usability (Requirement A) or minimalism (Requirement B) takes precedence, then revise one requirement accordingly.

Original_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, the first lines are shown.
Conflicting_FR: From the first titles of the news articles, no preview lines shall be shown.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended user experience by consulting stakeholders to determine whether preview lines (e.g., article summaries or first sentences) should be displayed alongside titles, and update one of the requirements accordingly.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable setting that allows toggling preview lines on or off, thereby satisfying both needs through personalization rather than a fixed system behavior.  
3. Refine the terminology in both requirements to eliminate ambiguity—e.g., distinguish between “first lines of the article body” and “title-only display”—to ensure they address different aspects and do not inherently contradict.

Original_FR: From the here the user can either proceed to call a rep for an order or proceed to the online checkout process
Conflicting_FR: From here, the user shall only be allowed to proceed to online checkout; calling a rep is disabled.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the user context or role: Allow calling a rep for certain user types (e.g., enterprise customers) while restricting it to online checkout only for others (e.g., standard users).  
2. Reconcile based on business rules: Define specific conditions (e.g., order value, product type, or inventory status) under which calling a rep is permitted versus when only online checkout is allowed.  
3. Update requirement documentation: Align stakeholders to choose one consistent behavior—either enable both options (revise Requirement B) or disable the call-a-rep option universally (revise Requirement A)—and document the decision.

Original_FR: From the here the user can either proceed to call a rep for an order or proceed to the online checkout process.
Conflicting_FR: From here, users shall only be allowed to call a rep; online checkout is disabled.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the user context or role: Allow online checkout for general users while restricting it (enabling only rep calls) for specific user types (e.g., new customers, high-value orders, or users in certain regions).  
2. Reconcile based on business rules: Define conditions under which each option is available (e.g., online checkout is disabled only during system maintenance or for out-of-stock items), and update both requirements accordingly.  
3. Align with stakeholder priorities: Engage product owners or business analysts to determine whether the primary goal is self-service efficiency (favoring Requirement A) or controlled sales support (favoring Requirement B), then revise one requirement to match the agreed strategy.

Original_FR: Full information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user name And password And address And e-mail address And phone number And mobile number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification
Conflicting_FR: If the restaurant owner selects the registration option, the system shall create a temporary account requiring only a user name and password, and defer the collection of address, e-mail, phone number, and mobile number until the verification application is initiated.
Resolution:
1. Align the registration flow by allowing initial account creation with only username and password (per Requirement B), but require all additional details (address, email, phone, mobile) at the point of verification application (satisfying Requirement A’s intent while deferring data collection).  
2. Update Requirement A to clarify that “full information” is required only when applying for verification—not during initial account creation—making it consistent with Requirement B’s staged data collection approach.  
3. Implement a two-step registration process: Step 1 creates a temporary account with minimal credentials; Step 2 (verification application) enforces completion of all required fields, ensuring both requirements are met without contradiction.

Original_FR: Full information for registration Given the restaurant owner wants to create an account And the restaurant owner does not have an account When the restaurant owner registers on the web-portal by providing user name And password And address And e-mail address And phone number And mobile number Then the restaurant owner should be able to apply for verification
Conflicting_FR: The restaurant owner must provide verification before registration is allowed; unverified accounts cannot be created.
Resolution:
1. Redefine the registration flow so that a preliminary account is created upon providing basic information (e.g., username, email), but full functionality—including applying for verification—is only unlocked after the restaurant owner submits required verification documents.  
2. Clarify terminology by distinguishing between “account creation” (a lightweight placeholder) and “verified registration” (full access), aligning both requirements to this two-step process.  
3. Update Requirement B to state that verification must be initiated during registration and completed within a defined timeframe, allowing initial account creation while enforcing eventual verification before full use.

Original_FR: Full news report is shown after choosing the corresponding news title.
Conflicting_FR: Full news reports shall only display summaries instead of the entire content.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of “full news report” in Requirement A to align with Requirement B—e.g., redefine it to mean a detailed summary rather than the complete original article.  
2. Introduce user-controlled access: display a summary by default (per Requirement B) but allow users to request the full original content via an explicit action (e.g., “View Full Article”), satisfying both requirements contextually.  
3. Differentiate content types or sources—apply Requirement B only to third-party or licensed content where full display is restricted, while allowing full display for internally produced news (addressing Requirement A).

Original_FR: Full news report is shown after choosing the corresponding news title.
Conflicting_FR: Only a summary of the news report shall be displayed instead of the full content.
Resolution:
1. Implement a two-tier display: show a summary by default, and provide a clear "Read Full Article" link or button to access the full news report upon user request.  
2. Allow user preferences or role-based settings (e.g., casual readers see summaries; registered users or journalists see full reports) to determine the level of detail displayed.  
3. Clarify stakeholder intent by revisiting the requirements with product owners to determine whether the conflict stems from differing user needs or miscommunication, then consolidate into a single coherent requirement.

Original_FR: Functionalities related to Electronic Programming Guide and DRM management shall be implemented in STB or media gateway and not in CNG.
Conflicting_FR: Functionalities related to EPG and DRM shall be implemented exclusively in CNG, not in STB or media gateway.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system architecture and ownership boundaries by convening stakeholders to agree on whether EPG and DRM functionalities should reside in the CNG or in the STB/media gateway, then update both requirements consistently.  
2. Refactor the requirements to allow for a hybrid implementation (e.g., core DRM/EPG logic in CNG with lightweight client-side components in STB/media gateway), ensuring roles and responsibilities are explicitly defined.  
3. Conduct a technical feasibility and security assessment to determine the optimal placement of EPG and DRM functions, using the findings to justify and align both requirements with architectural best practices.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall include the designation of the heating or cooling unit and a flag to indicate whether that unit is to be turned on or off.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall exclude the flag indicating ON/OFF status and only include unit designation.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system’s intended functionality with stakeholders to determine whether the ON/OFF flag is necessary; update both requirements to reflect the agreed behavior.  
2. If both use cases are valid (e.g., different modes or user roles), introduce separate request types—one with the flag and one without—and revise the requirements accordingly.  
3. Define a default behavior (e.g., absence of the flag implies “OFF”) and modify Requirement B to allow optional inclusion of the flag, aligning it with Requirement A.

Original_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall recognize the values for the requested heating or cooling unit and the ON or OFF status that is being requested. Since the interface to control the units has not been defined, the part of this process that will issue the signal is designated as TBD (to be determined).
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Signal shall not process any values until the interface is fully defined; signals shall not be issued for undefined units.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement A to explicitly state that signal generation is conditional on the interface being defined, aligning it with Requirement B’s constraint.  
2. Define a placeholder or mock interface for development and testing purposes, allowing partial implementation while ensuring no signals are sent to actual undefined units.  
3. Introduce a validation step in Generate_H/C_Signal that checks whether the target unit and interface are defined before processing any values, thereby satisfying both recognition (Requirement A) and safety (Requirement B) concerns.

Original_FR: geo-location feature to be able to find a user
Conflicting_FR: The geo-location feature shall not track any user location to protect privacy.
Resolution:
1. Implement a user-consented, on-demand geo-location sharing feature that only activates when the user explicitly chooses to share their location (e.g., during an emergency or specific service request), ensuring no background tracking occurs.  
2. Design the system with privacy-by-default settings: location data is never stored or transmitted unless actively initiated by the user, and all usage is transparently logged and auditable by the user.  
3. Introduce role-based access controls so that only authorized personnel (e.g., emergency responders) can request location data, and even then, only after obtaining real-time user consent.

Original_FR: GPS Connection DESC: The application should be connected to the GPS device.
Conflicting_FR: The application shall function without GPS connection; GPS is optional.
Resolution:
1. Revise Requirement A to clarify that GPS connection is required only for specific features (e.g., location-based services), not for the entire application.  
2. Update Requirement B to explicitly state which core functionalities must remain available without GPS, ensuring alignment with Requirement A’s scope.  
3. Introduce a conditional requirement: "If GPS is available and enabled, the application shall connect to it; otherwise, it shall operate in a degraded mode without GPS-dependent features."

Original_FR: Icons are according to the Android Icon Guidelines.
Conflicting_FR: Icons shall be customized arbitrarily and not follow Android Icon Guidelines.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the product scope: Determine whether the application is intended for general Android distribution (favoring Requirement A) or for a specialized/custom environment where branding overrides platform guidelines (favoring Requirement B).  
2. Adopt a hybrid approach: Follow Android Icon Guidelines for system-level or standard UI icons while allowing custom-designed icons for brand-specific or app-unique elements, clearly documenting which icons fall into each category.  
3. Engage stakeholders to prioritize: Facilitate a discussion with designers, developers, and product owners to align on whether platform consistency or unique visual identity is more critical, then revise one of the requirements accordingly.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications only for critical events; all other events are ignored.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of Requirement A by specifying that “all events” refers only to critical events, aligning it with Requirement B.  
2. Introduce a configurable notification policy in the UIMiddleware that allows clients to choose between receiving all events or only critical events, thereby satisfying both requirements through user preference.  
3. Refine Requirement A to apply only to a specific class of clients (e.g., administrators) while Requirement B applies to general users, differentiating behavior by user role.

Original_FR: If a client registers for notifications, the UIMiddleware shall send notifications for specific events.
Conflicting_FR: The UIMiddleware shall send notifications for all events regardless of registration preferences.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of "all events" in Requirement B—limit it to only those events for which the client has registered, aligning it with Requirement A.  
2. Introduce a system configuration flag that allows administrators to choose between per-client registration (Requirement A) or broadcast-all mode (Requirement B), making the behavior configurable rather than contradictory.  
3. Refine Requirement B to apply only to critical system-wide events (e.g., security alerts), while non-critical events follow the registration model in Requirement A, thereby differentiating event types and resolving the conflict.

Original_FR: If a command is issued by the VaadinUI for an UAV, the VaadinUI shall request control of that UAV.
Conflicting_FR: VaadinUI shall not request control of the UAV; commands are executed automatically by the UAV system.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system architecture: Determine whether the UAV system operates autonomously or requires explicit control delegation; align both requirements with the chosen operational model.  
2. Revise Requirement A to specify that the VaadinUI only issues advisory commands (not direct control), removing the need to request UAV control.  
3. Modify Requirement B to allow control requests from the VaadinUI in specific contexts (e.g., manual override mode), while maintaining automatic execution as the default behavior.

Original_FR: If a flight plan is active and the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the FlightManager shall notify the UAV to continue to the originally designated waypoint.
Conflicting_FR: If the "hover in place" command is rescinded, the UAV shall remain hovering until manual override is performed.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the precedence of commands: Define that an active flight plan takes priority over hover mode, so rescinding "hover in place" automatically resumes the flight plan without requiring manual override.  
2. Introduce a configurable behavior setting: Allow the operator to choose whether the UAV resumes the flight plan or remains hovering after "hover in place" is rescinded, based on mission context.  
3. Refine Requirement B to apply only when no active flight plan exists, ensuring consistency with Requirement A when a flight plan is active.

Original_FR: If a flight route is assigned to a UAV which is currently hovering in place, the FlightManager shall assign the route and maintain the hover in place mode.
Conflicting_FR: When a flight route is assigned, the UAV shall immediately exit hover mode and follow the assigned route.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior by specifying that "assigning a route" includes an explicit command to transition from hover mode, making Requirement B override Requirement A in such cases.  
2. Modify Requirement A to state that the UAV shall maintain hover mode only until the route execution is explicitly initiated, at which point it transitions per Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a new requirement defining a two-step process: route assignment (without immediate action) followed by a separate “execute route” command that triggers exit from hover mode, thereby reconciling both behaviors.

Original_FR: If a lead is scored to the high range the system will supply the lead data and score to the enterprise system.
Conflicting_FR: High-range lead data shall not be transmitted to the enterprise system for confidentiality reasons.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "high-range lead" in both requirements to ensure consistent interpretation—possibly distinguishing between different tiers (e.g., "high" vs. "very high") where only certain tiers are restricted.  
2. Modify Requirement A to exclude transmission of sensitive fields, allowing only non-confidential lead data and the score to be sent to the enterprise system, thereby satisfying confidentiality concerns in Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a conditional rule: transmit high-range lead data only when explicit consent or a valid business justification (e.g., sales team assignment) is present, aligning operational needs with confidentiality constraints.

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question in the response.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intent behind each requirement with stakeholders to determine whether examples should be included for misunderstood questions, and update one requirement to align with the agreed policy.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable setting that allows control over whether example questions are shown when a query is not understood, satisfying both requirements through conditional behavior.  
3. Refine the scope of Requirement A to apply only to specific API endpoints or user roles, while keeping Requirement B as the default behavior for all other cases.

Original_FR: If a question is not understood by our API, the system will send a text containing an example question after the text with the API response.
Conflicting_FR: If a question is not understood, the system shall not provide any example question.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intent behind each requirement with stakeholders to determine whether providing an example question is desirable in cases of misunderstood queries; align on a single behavior (either always provide or never provide examples).  
2. Introduce a configurable setting that allows users or administrators to choose whether example questions are shown when a query is not understood, thereby satisfying both requirements through user control.  
3. Refine the requirements by specifying distinct contexts (e.g., internal vs. external users, error severity levels) where one behavior applies over the other, eliminating direct conflict through contextual scoping.

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: If LO < = T <  UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended boundary condition at T = UO by consulting stakeholders to determine whether the upper bound should be inclusive or exclusive, then align both requirements accordingly.  
2. Revise one of the requirements to match the other’s boundary condition (e.g., update Requirement B to use “≤ UO” if the inclusive upper bound is correct, or update Requirement A to use “< UO” if the exclusive bound is intended).  
3. If both behaviors are needed in different contexts, split the requirement into two distinct cases with clear conditions (e.g., one for normal operation including UO and another for alarm or transition states excluding UO).

Original_FR: If LO < = T < = UO, then this process shall output the temperature status.
Conflicting_FR: The temperature status shall be output no more than once per second, even if T fluctuates rapidly within limits.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to include a time-based constraint, e.g., “If LO ≤ T ≤ UO for at least one second, then this process shall output the temperature status.”  
2. Clarify that Requirement B’s “no more than once per second” rule applies only when T remains within bounds, and allow an immediate output when T first enters the [LO, UO] range, followed by updates no more frequently than once per second.  
3. Introduce a debouncing mechanism: buffer rapid fluctuations and output the temperature status at most once per second, but ensure at least one output occurs whenever T enters or exits the valid range.

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: If T > LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended lower-bound condition: Determine whether the lower limit violation occurs when T < LO (Requirement A) or T > LO (Requirement B), and revise one requirement to align with the correct physical or operational logic.  
2. Standardize the logical expression across both requirements by confirming with domain experts that the correct condition for a temperature limit exceedance is “T < LO or T > UO,” then update both requirements accordingly.  
3. Add explicit definitions for LO (Lower Operating limit) and UO (Upper Operating limit) in the requirements specification to eliminate ambiguity and ensure consistent interpretation of boundary conditions.

Original_FR: If T < LO or UO < T then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded.
Conflicting_FR: The THEMAS system shall recognize temperature limit exceedance only if it persists for more than 3 consecutive seconds, preventing false alerts.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to include the persistence condition: "If T < LO or UO < T for more than 3 consecutive seconds, then the THEMAS system shall recognize this condition as the temperature limit has been exceeded."  
2. Clarify in Requirement B that the 3-second persistence rule applies only to alert generation, not to internal detection—allowing the system to detect limit exceedance immediately (per Requirement A) but delay alerts until the condition persists (per Requirement B).  
3. Introduce a two-tier recognition mechanism: immediate internal logging of any limit breach (satisfying Requirement A) and user-facing alerting only after 3 consecutive seconds (satisfying Requirement B).

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: If that thermostat requires no more heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior by revising Requirement B to state that the process shall submit a request to turn the unit **off** when no heating or cooling is needed, aligning it logically with Requirement A.  
2. Define explicit conditions (e.g., temperature thresholds or operational states) that determine when a unit should be turned on or off, and update both requirements to reference these unambiguous criteria.  
3. Consolidate the two requirements into a single, coherent requirement that specifies: “This process shall submit a request to turn the heating or cooling unit on if needed, and off otherwise,” eliminating contradictory phrasing.

Original_FR: If that thermostat still needs a heating or cooling unit turned on, this process shall submit a request to turn that unit on.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall submit the request only once per thermostat per heating/cooling cycle, and within 1 second of detecting the need.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to specify that the request is submitted only upon initial detection of a need, aligning it with Requirement B’s “once per cycle” constraint.  
2. Define “heating/cooling cycle” explicitly (e.g., from unit off to on and back to off) to ensure both requirements reference the same operational window.  
3. Add a condition to Requirement A stating that repeated requests are suppressed if a prior request for the same thermostat and cycle is already pending or fulfilled.

Original_FR: If the business rules allow this transaction is to be aggregated the PSAM must determine if it has an aggregation record for the card issuer. If the PSAM does not have an aggregation record for the card issuer a new aggregation record for the card issuer must be created
Conflicting_FR: The PSAM shall not create new aggregation records under any circumstances; missing records shall be ignored.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the business rules with stakeholders to determine whether aggregation record creation is ever permissible; if allowed, revise Requirement B to permit creation under specific conditions defined in Requirement A.  
2. If business policy strictly prohibits creating new aggregation records, update Requirement A to remove the mandate to create new records and instead specify that transactions requiring missing aggregation records must be rejected or logged as exceptions.  
3. Introduce a conditional exception in Requirement B (e.g., “except when explicitly authorized by business rules”) to align with Requirement A while maintaining control over when new records may be created.

Original_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC it shall support the AKA authentication method (TS 187 001 [8])
Conflicting_FR: If the CND contains an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support the AKA authentication method and shall use HTTP Digest instead.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the applicable standards or deployment scenarios: Determine whether the requirements apply to different contexts (e.g., 3GPP vs. non-3GPP networks) and revise each requirement to explicitly state its scope, ensuring they do not overlap.  
2. Align with the authoritative standard: Verify which authentication method is mandated by the referenced TS 187 001 [8] and update Requirement B to comply with that standard, removing the contradictory mandate for HTTP Digest.  
3. Introduce conditional logic: Modify the requirements to specify that AKA is used for 3GPP-based services while HTTP Digest is used for IMS or specific application-layer services, clearly delineating the conditions under which each method applies.

Original_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC it shall support the HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA
Conflicting_FR: If the CND does not contain an ISIM/UICC, it shall not support HTTP Digest authentication or the NBA.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended authentication policy with stakeholders to determine whether HTTP Digest/NBA support is mandatory or prohibited for CNDs without ISIM/UICC, then revise one of the requirements accordingly.  
2. Refine the scope or conditions of each requirement (e.g., by specifying different deployment scenarios, security levels, or device types) to eliminate direct contradiction while preserving valid use cases.  
3. Replace the conflicting statements with a single, unambiguous requirement that explicitly defines the allowed or required authentication methods for CNDs lacking ISIM/UICC.

Original_FR: If the DPU-SCUI FIFO is full when DPU-DPA attempts to enqueue its data product, DPU-DPA shall suspend event processing until room is available on the respective FIFO for the current data product. Incoming data may be dropped while suspended. An error shall be reported DPU-CCM.
Conflicting_FR: DPU-DPA shall continue processing events even if the FIFO is full, potentially overwriting incoming data.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system’s data integrity policy: decide whether data loss is acceptable (favoring Requirement B) or if backpressure with suspension is required (favoring Requirement A), and update both requirements to reflect the chosen policy consistently.  
2. Introduce a configurable operational mode that allows the system to switch between “lossless” (suspend on full FIFO per Requirement A) and “best-effort” (overwrite/continue per Requirement B) behaviors based on mission phase or operator command.  
3. Refine the requirements to apply different behaviors based on data priority—e.g., suspend for high-priority data (Requirement A) but allow overwriting for low-priority data (Requirement B)—ensuring both can coexist without contradiction.

Original_FR: if the item the user wants is available for exchange, the user can request the exchange of items that he/she has with the item he/she wants.
Conflicting_FR: Users shall not be allowed to request exchanges even if items are available.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the business rule: Determine whether exchanges are permitted under specific conditions (e.g., user role, item type, or system state) and revise both requirements to reflect those conditions consistently.  
2. Prioritize one requirement based on stakeholder input: If policy dictates that exchanges are never allowed, remove or reword Requirement A; if exchanges are a core feature, revise or remove Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a conditional clause: Modify Requirement B to allow exchanges only when explicitly permitted by system policy or user permissions, aligning it with the intent of Requirement A.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the high average score then it will be process by the ARA functionality.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with high average score shall be ignored by the ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of “high average score” to ensure both requirements use consistent terminology—either redefine the scoring thresholds or align the intended behavior for that segment.  
2. Reconcile the conflicting actions by specifying a priority rule (e.g., if Requirement B reflects a business policy to exclude certain leads, update Requirement A to exclude those leads explicitly).  
3. Consult stakeholders to determine the correct intended behavior for leads with a high average score and revise one of the requirements accordingly to eliminate the contradiction.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the low average then it will be returned to the supplying vendor
Conflicting_FR: Leads with low average score shall instead be processed by ARA functionality.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "low average score" to ensure both requirements refer to the same scoring threshold; if they differ, align them to a single agreed-upon range.  
2. Define a clear decision rule (e.g., priority or workflow sequence) specifying whether leads with a low average score are first returned to the vendor or processed by ARA—avoiding simultaneous contradictory actions.  
3. Introduce a conditional clause (e.g., “unless otherwise configured by business rules”) to allow configurable routing of low-average-score leads based on vendor agreements or operational context.

Original_FR: If the leads score falls within the medium average then it will be set for manual verification by an Enrollment Coordinator through the eleads system.
Conflicting_FR: Leads with medium average score shall be automatically approved without verification.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the definition of "medium average score" to ensure both requirements refer to the same scoring range; if they do, align the business rule by choosing either manual verification or automatic approval based on stakeholder priorities.  
2. Introduce a sub-categorization within the "medium average" score range (e.g., medium-low vs. medium-high) to allow automatic approval for higher-confidence leads and manual verification for lower-confidence ones.  
3. Consult key stakeholders (e.g., Enrollment Coordinators, product owners) to determine the intended risk tolerance and operational workflow, then update one or both requirements to reflect the agreed decision.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Additional cooling requests shall be rejected instead of being queued.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the system context: Determine whether the "maximum number of cooling units" scenario in Requirement A refers to a temporary operational limit (e.g., during maintenance) or a hard design capacity; if temporary, queuing may be acceptable, but if it reflects the system’s full capacity, align with Requirement B by rejecting requests.

2. Reconcile queuing policy with rejection rule: Modify Requirement A to specify that requests are only queued when the system is operating below absolute capacity but above a soft threshold; otherwise, enforce rejection per Requirement B.

3. Engage stakeholders to prioritize: Facilitate a discussion with system owners and users to decide whether reliability (rejecting excess load) or user experience (queuing for eventual service) is more critical, then update one requirement to reflect the agreed policy.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: Requests shall be queued only if the maximum number of units is not exceeded.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement B to specify that queuing occurs only when the maximum number of units is already running (i.e., "exceeded" should be revised to "reached"), aligning it with Requirement A’s condition for queuing.  
2. Replace “LIFO queue” in Requirement A with “queue” and define the queuing policy (LIFO or FIFO) separately in a design specification, ensuring the functional condition for queuing matches Requirement B once clarified.  
3. Reconcile terminology by updating both requirements to consistently use “maximum capacity reached” instead of “exceeded” to avoid ambiguity about whether queuing is allowed at the limit.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: If the maximum number of  cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Resolution:
1. Clarify whether Requirement A applies to heating and cooling units collectively or separately; if collectively, revise Requirement B to align with the shared limit and queue logic.  
2. If heating and cooling units have independent capacity limits, update Requirement A to explicitly state that it governs a combined or separate system, and ensure both requirements reference consistent queuing behavior per unit type.  
3. Define distinct LIFO queues for heating and cooling requests if they operate under separate maximum limits, and adjust both requirements to specify their respective scopes clearly.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is already running, the request will be added to a LIFO queue.
Conflicting_FR: The LIFO queue shall allow no more than 5 pending requests, rejecting additional requests until previous ones are processed.
Resolution:
1. Clarify that Requirement A only applies when the queue has fewer than 5 pending requests; otherwise, new requests are rejected per Requirement B.  
2. Modify Requirement A to specify that requests are added to the LIFO queue only if capacity remains, aligning it explicitly with the queue size limit in Requirement B.  
3. Introduce a system response (e.g., error code or notification) for rejected requests when the queue is full, ensuring both requirements coexist by defining behavior for both enqueued and rejected cases.

Original_FR: If the maximum number of heating or cooling units is not running, this process will generate a request to turn on the requested unit.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall never generate a request to turn on a unit, even if the maximum is not reached.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intent behind Requirement B—determine whether it applies universally or only under specific conditions (e.g., safety constraints, maintenance mode)—and revise it to include those conditions explicitly.  
2. Reconcile the requirements by modifying Requirement A to exclude scenarios where turning on a unit is prohibited, aligning with the constraints implied in Requirement B.  
3. Establish a priority or override mechanism (e.g., safety overrides operational logic) so that Requirement B takes precedence only when justified, while Requirement A governs normal operation.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall select all events and ignore any limit on the number of events in the report.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of each requirement by specifying that Requirement A applies only to the "operational history report" while Requirement B applies to other report types, ensuring no overlap in their contexts.  
2. Modify Requirement A to allow user-configurable control over the number of events (e.g., “select a few events by default, unless overridden”), aligning with Requirement B’s intent when needed.  
3. Update both requirements to include explicit conditions: e.g., “If the user requests a summary report, select a few events; if a full audit report is requested, select all events without limit.”

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select a few events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended scope of the operational history report with stakeholders to determine whether it should include all events or only a subset (e.g., recent, critical, or user-specified events), then revise one requirement accordingly.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable option that allows selection between a full event report (Requirement A) and a filtered/sampled report (Requirement B), thereby satisfying both needs through a single, flexible requirement.  
3. Define precise criteria for “a few events” in Requirement B (e.g., last 100 events, events within a date range, or high-priority events only) to eliminate ambiguity and assess whether it truly conflicts with Requirement A’s “all events” scope.

Original_FR: If the operational history report is selected, the THEMAS system shall select all the events from the event database, sort the events by date and time, and create the ASCII report file.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall only select a single event for the operational history report.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended scope of the operational history report with stakeholders: determine whether it should include all events (Requirement A) or only a single event (Requirement B), and revise one requirement accordingly.  
2. Introduce a user-configurable option in THEMAS that allows selection between a full operational history (all events) and a single-event report, thereby satisfying both needs through flexible functionality.  
3. Refine the terminology by distinguishing between different types of reports (e.g., “full operational history report” vs. “single-event operational summary”) to eliminate ambiguity and assign each requirement to its respective report type.

Original_FR: If the POS device supports multiple applications or multiple transaction types an interaction between the terminal and consumer or sales agent determines the CEP application and the function to be performed (cancel last purchase transaction)
Conflicting_FR: POS device shall always use the default application regardless of user interaction.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of Requirement B by specifying that the "default application" applies only when no explicit user interaction occurs, allowing Requirement A to govern cases where user input selects a different application or function.  
2. Modify Requirement B to permit override of the default application when a specific transaction type (e.g., “cancel last purchase”) is requested via user interaction, aligning with Requirement A’s intent.  
3. Define a priority hierarchy in the requirements specification stating that user-initiated actions (as in Requirement A) take precedence over default behavior (Requirement B) for supported transaction types.

Original_FR: If the queue is not empty, this process shall remove one request from the LIFO queue and check the current state of the thermostat for which the queued request was made.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall remove requests randomly instead of checking thermostat state.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the queue type and processing policy: Determine whether the system must use a LIFO queue (as in Requirement A) or random selection (as in Requirement B), and align both requirements to the same queue discipline—either enforce LIFO consistently or replace it with random selection throughout.

2. Separate concerns by introducing distinct processing modes: Allow the system to support both LIFO-based and random request processing, controlled by a configuration parameter or mode flag, ensuring each requirement applies only under its designated mode.

3. Reconcile the intent behind “checking thermostat state”: If the core need is to avoid unnecessary processing, modify Requirement B to allow random selection but still require thermostat state validation before acting on the selected request, preserving functional safety while accommodating randomness.

Original_FR: If the settings reflect a need for a heating or cooling unit to be turned on, the process shall issue a request to turn on the appropriate unit.
Conflicting_FR: The process shall not issue requests even if a unit needs to be turned on.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the conditions under which requests may be issued by revising Requirement B to specify exceptions (e.g., “The process shall not issue requests unless explicitly authorized by user settings or safety protocols”).  
2. Reconcile the requirements by introducing a priority or override mechanism—e.g., allow requests only when both a unit needs activation and a separate enable flag is set.  
3. Determine stakeholder intent through elicitation: confirm whether the system should ever autonomously trigger units, then revise one requirement to align with that decision.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a hit the product shall allow the offensive player to define a shot.
Conflicting_FR: The product shall prevent the offensive player from defining a shot even if the previous shot was a hit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the game rules with stakeholders to determine whether a hit should grant or deny the offensive player another shot, then revise one of the requirements accordingly.  
2. Introduce a conditional rule (e.g., “only if it’s not the final shot in a sequence”) to reconcile both requirements and eliminate ambiguity.  
3. Define distinct roles or contexts (e.g., “offensive player” vs. “defensive player”) more precisely to ensure the requirements apply to mutually exclusive scenarios.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a miss the product shall change the status of the defensive player to offensive
Conflicting_FR: The product shall keep the defensive player’s status unchanged even after a miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended game logic with stakeholders to determine whether a miss should trigger a role switch or maintain the current defensive status, then revise one of the requirements accordingly.  
2. Introduce a conditional rule (e.g., based on game mode or player configuration) that specifies when a miss changes the player’s status and when it does not, reconciling both requirements under different contexts.  
3. Define “miss” more precisely—distinguish between types of misses (e.g., shot clock violation vs. airball)—so that only specific miss scenarios trigger a status change, aligning both requirements with refined definitions.

Original_FR: If the shot was marked as a miss the product shall change the status of the offensive player to defensive
Conflicting_FR: The product shall not change the offensive player’s status even after a miss.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended game logic with stakeholders to determine whether a missed shot should trigger a status change; update one of the requirements accordingly.  
2. Introduce a conditional rule (e.g., only change status on a miss during specific game phases or modes) to reconcile both requirements contextually.  
3. Define distinct player roles or states (e.g., “active offensive” vs. “inactive offensive”) so that a miss changes a sub-state without violating the core offensive designation.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall present months instead of dates for statistical report selection.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the user need: Determine whether users require granularity at the date level or month level for statistical reports, and revise one requirement to align with the validated user need.  
2. Implement a hierarchical selection: Present months first (per Requirement B), then allow the operator to expand a selected month to view and choose specific dates (satisfying Requirement A).  
3. Differentiate report types: If different statistical reports have different granularity needs, specify in each requirement which report type it applies to, eliminating overlap and conflict.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present a list of available months instead of dates.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended granularity of the time selection with stakeholders—determine whether reports should be filtered by specific dates or by months—and update both requirements to reflect the agreed scope.  
2. Implement a hierarchical selection interface: first present available months, then allow the operator to drill down into specific dates within the selected month, satisfying both requirements through a combined approach.  
3. Define distinct report types (e.g., “daily statistical report” vs. “monthly statistical report”) so that each requirement applies to a different report subtype, eliminating direct conflict.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the reporting granularity needed by stakeholders: determine whether reports are intended to be monthly (Requirement A) or daily (Requirement B), and update both requirements to reflect the agreed scope.  
2. Combine both requirements into a hierarchical selection interface: first present available months, then allow the operator to select specific dates within the chosen month.  
3. Define distinct report types—e.g., “Monthly Statistical Report” and “Daily Statistical Report”—so each requirement applies to its respective report type, eliminating overlap.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: THEMAS shall present specific dates instead of months for selection.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the context of use: Determine whether the statistical report requires monthly aggregation (supporting Requirement A) or daily granularity (supporting Requirement B), and align both requirements to the same reporting level.  
2. Implement a hierarchical selection interface: Allow the operator to first select a month (per Requirement A) and then drill down to specific dates within that month (satisfying Requirement B).  
3. Differentiate by report type: Specify that monthly selection applies to high-level statistical reports (Requirement A), while detailed reports use date-based selection (Requirement B), ensuring each requirement applies to distinct use cases.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available dates from which to make a selection.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the reporting granularity needed by stakeholders: determine whether reports are intended to be monthly (Requirement A) or daily (Requirement B), and update both requirements to reflect the agreed scope.  
2. Combine both requirements into a hierarchical selection interface: first present available months, then allow the operator to drill down to specific dates within the selected month.  
3. Define distinct report types—e.g., “Monthly Statistical Report” and “Daily Statistical Report”—so each requirement applies to its respective report type, eliminating overlap.

Original_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present the operator with a list of available months from which to make a selection.
Conflicting_FR: If the statistical report is selected, the THEMAS system shall present a list of available dates instead of months.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended granularity of the statistical report with stakeholders—determine whether reports should be organized by month or by individual dates—and update one requirement to align with the agreed scope.  
2. Introduce a hierarchical selection interface: first present available months, then allow the operator to drill down into specific dates within the selected month, thereby satisfying both requirements through a combined workflow.  
3. Define distinct report types (e.g., “Monthly Summary” vs. “Daily Detail”) so that each requirement applies to a different report subtype, eliminating direct conflict while preserving both functionalities.

Original_FR: If the system detects a temperature limit has been exceeded, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 1000 Hz and 1500 Hz beeps on the supervisor???s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a single continuous tone instead of alternating beeps.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the alarm type based on severity: use alternating beeps for critical temperature breaches (per Requirement A) and a single continuous tone for less urgent alarms (aligning with Requirement B).  
2. Revise both requirements to specify distinct alarm conditions—e.g., alternating tones for hardware-level temperature faults and a single tone for software-detected anomalies—ensuring non-overlapping triggers.  
3. Consult stakeholders to determine the preferred alarm behavior and update one requirement to supersede the other, ensuring consistency in the system’s audible alert protocol.

Original_FR: If the system detects a temperature limit has been exceeded, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 1000 Hz and 1500 Hz beeps on the supervisor’s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a single continuous tone instead of alternating beeps.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the alarm context: Define distinct alarm types (e.g., temperature exceedance vs. other critical events) and assign alternating beeps to one type and a single tone to another, ensuring each requirement applies to a specific scenario.  
2. Prioritize based on user needs: Consult stakeholders to determine which auditory pattern is preferred for temperature-limit alarms and revise the lower-priority requirement accordingly.  
3. Introduce configurability: Modify Generate_Alarm_Data to support both alarm patterns as configurable options, allowing system administrators to select the appropriate behavior per deployment environment.

Original_FR: If the system detects an invalid temperature, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 500 Hz and 700 Hz beeps on the supervisor?‘s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output 1000 Hz constant tone instead of 500/700 Hz alternating signal.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the triggering conditions: Define distinct scenarios under which each alarm tone is used (e.g., 500/700 Hz for invalid temperature, 1000 Hz for other critical faults), ensuring mutually exclusive contexts.  
2. Update one requirement to supersede the other based on stakeholder priority—e.g., if the 1000 Hz tone is preferred for all alarms, revise Requirement A accordingly.  
3. Introduce a configuration parameter or user setting that allows the system to switch between alarm types based on operational mode or user role, satisfying both needs contextually.

Original_FR: If the system detects an invalid temperature, Generate_Alarm_Data shall output a continuous series of alternating 500 Hz and 700 Hz beeps on the supervisor’s computer.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_Alarm_Data shall output no alarm signal for invalid temperature.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended behavior with stakeholders: Determine whether an alarm should be generated for invalid temperature readings, and revise either Requirement A or B accordingly based on the agreed-upon system behavior.  
2. Refine the requirements to distinguish between types of “invalid temperature” (e.g., sensor failure vs. out-of-range values), allowing alarms for some cases and silence for others.  
3. Introduce a configuration parameter or operational mode that allows the system to switch between alarming and non-alarming behavior for invalid temperatures, satisfying both requirements contextually.

Original_FR: if the system unable to update the location , Show failure msg and return the user to home page.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall silently fail and remain on the current page without any message.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the user context and failure severity: Define specific conditions under which each behavior applies (e.g., silent failure for non-critical background updates vs. explicit error for user-initiated location updates).  
2. Introduce a configurable error-handling policy: Allow system administrators or users to choose between silent failure and explicit messaging based on their preferences or use case.
