2. If thermostat context is necessary for auditability or diagnostics, update Requirement B to include thermostat designation, ensuring consistency with Requirement A.  
3. Introduce a configurable event logging level where basic logs follow Requirement B (minimal data) and detailed logs follow Requirement A (full context), allowing flexibility based on deployment needs.

Original_FR: This unit will receive a text string from the URL parameter. 1 This unit will identify important words in the sentence and replace them with generic representations preceded by an escape character. (example:  ??PISB  becomes  ??$building )  1This unit will output the sentence as a string, as described in R4.  1This unit will output a map of generic representations to the words they replaced.
Conflicting_FR: If the user query contains the text string as a URL-encoded form parameter in the request body, the unit shall parse the form data, extract the text string, and perform the substitution process.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the input source precedence: Specify whether the system should prioritize the text string from the URL parameter (Requirement A) or from the URL-encoded form parameter in the request body (Requirement B) when both are present, or define mutually exclusive usage scenarios.

2. Unify input handling logic: Modify the unit to accept the text string from either source (URL parameter or form body) using a single parsing interface, ensuring consistent substitution behavior regardless of input origin.

3. Update both requirements to explicitly state support for multiple input methods: Revise Requirement A to acknowledge alternative input sources and Requirement B to align with the output format (generic representations and mapping) defined in Requirement A.

Original_FR: This unit will receive as input a generic answer from the Generic Answer Construction unit
Conflicting_FR: If the generic answer is unavailable, the unit shall source a default response template from the local configuration database.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to specify that the input from the Generic Answer Construction unit is conditional—i.e., "This unit will receive as input a generic answer from the Generic Answer Construction unit, if available."  
2. Explicitly define precedence or fallback logic in the system design: the unit first attempts to use the generic answer; only upon confirmed unavailability does it retrieve the default template from the local configuration database.  
3. Add a validation step in the interface contract stating that the absence of a generic answer must be signaled unambiguously (e.g., via a null value or error code) to trigger the fallback behavior described in Requirement B.

Original_FR: This unit will receive as input a mapping from the Generic Question Construction unit
Conflicting_FR: This unit will receive as input a formatted query object from the Natural Language Parser unit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the data flow by specifying that the Generic Question Construction unit produces a formatted query object that is then passed through the Natural Language Parser unit, ensuring both requirements describe sequential rather than competing inputs.  
2. Refactor the system architecture so that this unit accepts a unified input interface that can handle either source, with an adapter or mediator component normalizing inputs from both the Generic Question Construction and Natural Language Parser units.  
3. Update the requirements to explicitly define that the formatted query object from the Natural Language Parser unit is derived from (or includes) the mapping provided by the Generic Question Construction unit, thereby aligning their roles in the pipeline.

Original_FR: user can add the items he/she likes to his cart by touching a Add to the carta button.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall add items to the user's cart only after the user has long-pressed the item image for three seconds.
Resolution:
1. Align the interaction method by replacing the “Add to cart” button with a long-press gesture on the item image, ensuring both requirements describe the same user action.  
2. Support both interaction methods (button tap and long-press) as alternative ways to add items to the cart, clarifying in the requirements that either is acceptable.  
3. Define context-specific triggers—e.g., use the “Add to cart” button on product detail pages and require long-press only in browsing/grid views—to avoid direct conflict while maintaining usability.

Original_FR:
user enters his/her username or email and his/her password and logs in the
system
Conflicting_FR: The user shall log in to the system using a one-time passcode sent to their registered mobile device.
Resolution:
1. Implement a unified login interface that supports both traditional credential-based authentication (username/email + password) and one-time passcode (OTP) authentication, allowing users to choose their preferred method.  
2. Use adaptive authentication: require OTP only under specific risk conditions (e.g., new device or location), while permitting standard password login otherwise.  
3. Deprecate password-based login in favor of OTP-only authentication if security policy mandates it, and update Requirement A accordingly to align with Requirement B.

Original_FR: User registration - Mobile application DESC: Given that a user has downloaded the mobile application, then the user should be able to register through the mobile application. The user must provide user-name, password and e-mail address. The user can choose to provide a regularly used phone number.
Conflicting_FR: "User registration - Mobile application DESC: Given that a user has downloaded the mobile application, then the user should be able to register through the mobile application. The user must provide user-name, password, and regularly used phone number. The user can choose to provide an e-mail address."
Resolution:
1. Harmonize the mandatory fields by requiring both email address and phone number during registration, ensuring consistency and maximizing user contact options.  
2. Make both email and phone number optional but require at least one of them to be provided, balancing flexibility with the need for user contact information.  
3. Clarify business or regulatory needs (e.g., SMS verification vs. email confirmation) to determine which field is truly mandatory, then update both requirements accordingly.

Original_FR: When a request to turn on a heating or cooling unit is received, the system shall determine if the request can be honored.
Conflicting_FR: When a request to turn on a heating  unit is received, the system shall determine if the request can be honored.
Resolution:
1. Merge the two requirements into a single, unified requirement that explicitly covers both heating and cooling units to eliminate redundancy and ensure consistency.  
2. Clarify the scope of Requirement A to include only cooling units (or units not otherwise specified), while keeping Requirement B specific to heating units, ensuring no overlap.  
3. Add a note or rationale stating that Requirement B is a specific instance of Requirement A, and maintain both only if hierarchical traceability is needed—otherwise, retain only the more general Requirement A.

Original_FR: When a request to turn on a heating or cooling unit is received, the system shall determine if the request can be honored.
Conflicting_FR: The system shall determine request feasibility within 2 seconds, and must not honor more than one request simultaneously per heating unit.
Resolution:
1. Clarify Requirement A to explicitly state that honoring a request is conditional on both feasibility (within 2 seconds per Requirement B) and the unit’s availability (i.e., no concurrent requests).  
2. Modify Requirement B to specify that the 2-second feasibility check includes verifying unit availability, ensuring alignment with Requirement A’s intent to conditionally honor requests.  
3. Introduce a queuing mechanism in the system design that defers non-feasible or concurrent requests, allowing Requirement A to “determine if the request can be honored” while adhering to Requirement B’s concurrency and timing constraints.

Original_FR: When a request to turn on or off a heating or cooling unit is made, Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request to carry out that request.
Conflicting_FR: When a request to turn off a heating or cooling unit is made, Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request to carry out that request
Resolution:
1. Merge the two requirements into a single, unified requirement that explicitly covers both turning on and turning off heating or cooling units to eliminate redundancy and ensure consistency.  
2. Clarify the scope of Requirement B to address only turn-off scenarios if there is a distinct operational difference (e.g., safety checks) for turning off versus turning on, and update Requirement A to exclude turn-off actions.  
3. Add a note or constraint specifying that Requirement B takes precedence for turn-off requests, while Requirement A applies only to turn-on requests, ensuring unambiguous behavior without overlap.

Original_FR: When a request to turn on or off a heating or cooling unit is made, Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the appropriate request to carry out that request.
Conflicting_FR: Generate_H/C_Request shall generate the shutdown request within 500 milliseconds, and only if the unit has been on for at least 1 second to avoid rapid toggling.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to explicitly exclude rapid toggling scenarios, aligning it with the safety constraint in Requirement B (e.g., "…provided the unit has been on for at least 1 second in the case of a shutdown request").  
2. Update the system design so that Generate_H/C_Request includes a debouncing mechanism that enforces the 1-second minimum on-time before allowing a shutdown, ensuring both functional intent and timing constraints are met.  
3. Clarify in the requirements specification that the 500 ms response time in Requirement B applies only when the 1-second on-time condition is satisfied, making the timing constraint conditional rather than absolute.

Original_FR: When an evidence item of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence item in red.
Conflicting_FR: When an evidence item of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the external tool is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence item in red.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the trigger condition by distinguishing visual indicators: use red to signify modifications made within OPENCOSS and a different color (e.g., orange) to indicate changes in the external tool.  
2. Introduce a combined indicator (e.g., red with an icon or border) that shows both sources of modification, along with a tooltip or metadata explaining the origin and nature of each change.  
3. Refine the requirements to specify that red coloring applies only when the local copy in OPENCOSS diverges from the latest version in the external tool, regardless of which system initiated the change, ensuring consistency in visual semantics.

Original_FR: When an evidence item of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence item in red.
Conflicting_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall indicate the modification by colouring the evidence item in red within 1 second of the external tool update, and only once per modification.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the trigger for red coloring: specify that Requirement A applies only to user-initiated modifications within OPENCOSS, while Requirement B applies exclusively to updates originating from the external tool.  
2. Harmonize timing and frequency: update Requirement A to include the 1-second response time and “once per modification” constraint from Requirement B to ensure consistent behavior regardless of modification source.  
3. Introduce distinct visual indicators: use red coloring for internal modifications (per Requirement A) and a different indicator (e.g., flashing red or border highlight) for external-tool updates (per Requirement B) to avoid ambiguity while preserving both intents.

Original_FR: When an evidence traceability link of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence traceability link in red.
Conflicting_FR: When an evidence traceability link of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the external tool is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence traceability link in red.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the scope of each requirement by specifying distinct visual indicators (e.g., red for external-tool changes, orange for local OPENCOSS changes) to differentiate the source of modifications.  
2. Introduce a combined status indicator that shows both modification sources (e.g., red with an icon or tooltip indicating whether the change originated externally or internally).  
3. Refine the requirements to define a synchronization protocol: only highlight in red when the local copy diverges from the latest external version, treating internal edits as temporary until reconciliation.

Original_FR: When an evidence traceability link of an assurance project contains information imported from an external tool, and the information in the OPENCOSS platform is modified, the OPENCOSS platform shall indicate such a modification by colouring the evidence traceability link in red.
Conflicting_FR: The OPENCOSS platform shall colour the evidence traceability link in red no more than once every 5 seconds, regardless of multiple modifications in the external tool.
Resolution:
1. Modify Requirement A to specify that the red colouring should only be applied if the modification has not already been indicated within the last 5 seconds, aligning it with Requirement B’s rate-limiting constraint.  
2. Update Requirement B to exclude modifications made directly in the OPENCOSS platform from the 5-second cooldown, ensuring immediate visual feedback for local changes as required by Requirement A.  
3. Introduce a separate visual indicator (e.g., blinking or a badge) for rapid successive modifications while retaining red colouring for the first change within each 5-second window, satisfying both visibility and rate-limiting needs.

Original_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in red.
Conflicting_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in blue.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended visual semantics with stakeholders to determine whether "pending" actions should convey urgency (typically red) or informational status (often blue), and update one requirement accordingly.  
2. Introduce a more granular status model (e.g., “pending – urgent” vs. “pending – routine”) to allow distinct color coding that satisfies both intent and usability needs.  
3. Replace color-only indicators with combined visual cues (e.g., color + icon or label) to reduce ambiguity and ensure accessibility, then align both requirements to this enhanced design.

Original_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in red.
Conflicting_FR: When the status of an action required in an assurance project as a result of evidence evaluation is "pending", the OPENCOSS platform shall colour such an action in blue.
Resolution:
1. Clarify the intended visual semantics with stakeholders to determine whether "pending" actions should convey urgency (typically red) or neutrality/information (typically blue), and align both requirements accordingly.  
2. Introduce a more granular status model (e.g., "pending – urgent" vs. "pending – routine") to allow distinct color coding based on sub-status, thereby satisfying both intent and usability needs.  
3. Replace absolute color assignments with configurable or user-defined color schemes, enabling project-specific customization while maintaining consistency within each project context.

Original_FR: When the THEMAS system detects a request for an alarm, Generate_Alarm_Data shall detect which of the two alarms are being requested.
Conflicting_FR: When the THEMAS system detects a request for an alarm, Generate_Alarm_Data shall store which of the two alarms are being requested.
Resolution:
1. Merge the two requirements into a single, cohesive requirement that specifies both detection and storage as sequential actions: “When the THEMAS system detects a request for an alarm, Generate_Alarm_Data shall detect and store which of the two alarms are being requested.”  
2. Clarify the scope of each requirement by assigning distinct responsibilities—e.g., let Requirement A define the detection logic, and Requirement B specify that the result of that detection must be persisted, ensuring they are complementary rather than conflicting.  
3. Add traceability or dependency notation indicating that Requirement B depends on the output of Requirement A, thereby establishing an ordered execution flow that resolves any ambiguity about their relationship.
